2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
100	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
101	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
102	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
103	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
104	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
105	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
106	    \begin{quote}
107	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
108	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
109	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
111	    \end{quote}
112	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
113	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
114	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
115	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
116	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
117	    properness; integrality threaded).
118	\end{itemize}
119	\noindent
120	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
121	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
122	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
123	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
124	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
125	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
126	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
127	\begin{definition}
128	\leanok
129	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
130	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
132	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
133	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
134	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
135	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
136	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
137	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
138	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
139	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
140	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
141	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
142	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
143	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
144	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
146	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
147	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
148	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
149	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
150	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
151	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
152	  the Mathlib predicate
153	  \[
154	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
155	  \]
156	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
157	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
158	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
159	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
160	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
161	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
162	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
163	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
164	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
165	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
166	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
167	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
168	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
169	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
171	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
172	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
173	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
174	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
175	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
176	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
177	  \begin{itemize}
178	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
179	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
180	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
181	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
182	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
183	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
184	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
185	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
186	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
187	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
188	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
189	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
190	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
191	      uses.
192	  \end{itemize}
193	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
194	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
195	  needs.
196	\end{definition}
197	\begin{definition}
198	\leanok
199	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
200	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
201	  
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
203	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
204	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
205	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
206	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
208	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
209	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
210	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
211	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
213	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
214	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
215	  \[
216	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
217	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
218	  \]
219	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
220	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
221	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
222	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
223	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
224	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
225	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
226	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
227	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
228	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
229	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
230	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
231	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
232	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
233	\end{definition}
234	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
238	  \label{def:order_at_point}
239	  
240	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
242	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
243	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
244	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
245	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
247	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
248	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
249	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
250	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
251	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
252	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
253	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
254	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
255	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
256	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
257	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
258	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
259	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
260	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
261	  \[
262	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
263	  \]
264	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
265	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
266	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
267	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
268	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
269	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
270	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
271	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
272	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
273	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
274	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
275	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
276	  \begin{quote}
277	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
278	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
279	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
280	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
281	  \end{quote}
282	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
283	  see below). The body is
284	  \[
285	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
286	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
287	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
288	      \bigr).
289	  \]
290	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
291	  \begin{itemize}
292	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
293	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
294	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
295	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
296	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
297	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
298	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
299	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
300	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
301	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
302	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
303	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
304	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
305	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
306	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
307	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
308	      X.functionField} via
309	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
310	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
311	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
312	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
313	      and from the explicitly threaded
314	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
315	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
316	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
317	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
318	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
319	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
320	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
321	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
322	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
323	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
324	  \end{itemize}
325	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
326	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
327	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
328	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
329	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
330	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
331	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
332	  signature.
333	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
334	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
335	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
336	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
337	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
338	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
339	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
340	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
341	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
342	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
343	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
344	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
345	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
346	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
347	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
348	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
349	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
350	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
351	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
352	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
353	\end{definition}
354	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
355	\begin{definition}
356	\leanok
357	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
358	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
359	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
360	  
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
362	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
363	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
364	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
365	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
366	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
367	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
368	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
369	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
370	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
371	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
372	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
373	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
374	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
375	  \[
376	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
377	  \]
378	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
379	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
380	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
381	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
382	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
383	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
384	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
385	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
386	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
387	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
388	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
389	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
390	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
391	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
392	  codim-\(1\) regime:
393	  \[
394	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
395	      \begin{cases}
396	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
397	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
398	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
399	      \end{cases}
400	  \]
401	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
402	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
403	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
404	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
405	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
406	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
407	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
408	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
409	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
410	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
411	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
412	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
413	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
414	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
415	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
416	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
417	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
418	\end{definition}
419	\begin{lemma}
420	\leanok
421	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-\(1\) regime]
422	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
424	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
425	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
426	  \(h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\) (the codimension-one witness
427	  promoting \(P\) to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}),
428	  then
429	  \[
430	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
431	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
432	      \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
433	  \]
434	\end{lemma}
435	\begin{proof}
436	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
437	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
438	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
439	\end{proof}
440	\begin{lemma}
441	\leanok
442	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
443	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
445	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
446	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
447	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\) (the off-branch: \(P\) is not a
448	  codimension-one point of \(C\) --- e.g.\ \(P\) is the generic point of an
449	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
450	  scheme), then
451	  \[
452	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
453	      0 \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
454	  \]
455	\end{lemma}
456	\begin{proof}
457	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
458	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
459	  negative branch using the hypothesis
460	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\).
461	\end{proof}
462	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
463	\begin{definition}
464	\leanok
465	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
466	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
467	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
468	  
469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
470	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
471	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
472	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
473	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
474	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
475	  nonsingular curve).}
476	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
477	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
478	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
479	  \[
480	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
481	  \]
482	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
483	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
484	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
485	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
486	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
487	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
488	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
489	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
490	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
491	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
492	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
493	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
494	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
495	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
496	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
497	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
498	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
499	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
500	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
501	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
502	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
503	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
504	\end{definition}
505	\begin{theorem}
506	\leanok
507	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
508	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
510	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
511	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
512	  homomorphism
513	  \[
514	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
515	    \qquad
516	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
517	    \qquad
518	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
519	  \]
520	\end{theorem}
521	\begin{proof}
522	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the free abelian group on the
523	  set of closed points of \(C\), and \(\deg\) is the unique group homomorphism
524	  sending each generator \([P]\) to \(1 \in \mathbb Z\) (equivalently: the sum
525	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
526	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
527	\end{proof}
528	\section{Principal divisors}
529	\begin{definition}
530	\leanok
531	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
532	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
533	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
534	  
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
536	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of \(\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)\),
537	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
538	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
539	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
540	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
541	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\) and let \(f \in K^\ast\).
542	  % We define the \emph{divisor of \(f\)}, denoted \((f)\), by
543	  % \((f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y\), where the sum is taken over all prime
544	  % divisors \(Y\) of \(X\). By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
545	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
546	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
547	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
548	  p.~131.}
549	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\),
550	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that \(\ord_Y(f) = 0\) for all but finitely
551	  many prime divisors \(Y\) on \(X\); hence the formal sum
552	  \[
553	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
554	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X)
555	  \]
556	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
557	  \emph{principal divisor of \(f\)}. On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\)
558	  the same definition specialises to
559	  \(\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]\), the formal
560	  sum over closed points.
561	\end{definition}
562	\begin{theorem}
563	\leanok
564	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
565	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
566	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
567	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
568	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
569	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
570	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if \(f, g \in K^\ast\), then \((f/g) = (f) - (g)\)
571	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
572	  % \(f\) to its divisor \((f)\) gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
573	  % group \(K^\ast\) to the additive group \(\operatorname{Div} X\), and the
574	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
575	  % \(\operatorname{Div} X\)."
576	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
577	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map
578	  \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the
579	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
580	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
581	  \[
582	    \mathrm{div}(fg) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g),
583	    \qquad
584	    \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\mathrm{div}(f),
585	    \qquad
586	    \mathrm{div}(1) \;=\; 0.
587	  \]
588	\end{theorem}
589	\begin{proof}
590	  Each prime divisor \(Y\) on \(X\) contributes a discrete valuation \(v_Y\) on
591	  \(K(X)\) (\ref{def:principal_divisor}); by the standard DVR axioms,
592	  \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\) and \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\) for $f, g \in
593	  K(X)^{\times}\(, and \)v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
594	  divisors of \(X\) (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
595	  \ref{thm:principal_hom}, so the support of the sum is the union
596	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
597	  identities.
598	\end{proof}
599	\begin{theorem}
600	\leanok
601	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
602	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
603	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
604	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
605	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
606	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
607	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
608	  % nonsingular curve \(X\) has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
609	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
610	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
611	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
612	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
613	  \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) the
614	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
615	  \[
616	    \deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
617	  \]
618	\end{theorem}
619	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let \(f \in K(X)^\ast\). If \(f \in k\), then
620	% \((f) = 0\), so there is nothing to prove. If \(f \notin k\), then the
621	% inclusion of fields \(k(f) \subset K(X)\) induces a finite morphism
622	% \(\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1\). It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
623	% have \((f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})\). Since \(\{0\} - \{\infty\}\)
624	% is a divisor of degree 0 on \(\mathbf P^1\), we conclude that \((f)\) has
625	% degree 0 on \(X\)."
626	\begin{proof}
627	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If \(f \in \bar k \subset K(C)\) is
628	  a constant scalar, then \(f\) has no zeros or poles, so \(\mathrm{div}(f) = 0\) and
629	  \(\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = 0\). Otherwise the inclusion of fields
630	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) exhibits \(K(C)\) as a finite extension of the
631	  rational function field \(\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)\), so the corresponding
632	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is finite (by the
633	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
634	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
635	  divisor \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) equals the pullback \(\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])\)
636	  along \(\varphi\) of the degree-\(0\) divisor \([0] - [\infty]\) on
637	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\); since pullback along a finite morphism
638	  multiplies degrees by \(\deg(\varphi)\), we conclude
639	  $\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
640	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
641	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
642	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) defines a finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)''
643	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
644	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
645	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
646	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
647	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
648	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
649	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
650	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
651	\end{proof}
652	\section{Positive part of a Weil divisor}
653	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase): added to host the Pin 2 corrective per
654	% `task_results/AlgebraicJacobian_RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.lean.md`
655	% (iter-189). `Hom.poleDivisor` is being refactored to return the
656	% positive part of `principal (φ^* t_∞)` so that
657	% `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is mathematically correct (the principal
658	% divisor has degree 0 by `thm:principal_deg_zero`; only its
659	% positive part can have positive degree).
660	\begin{definition}
661	
662	
663	\leanok
664	[Positive part of a Weil divisor]
665	  \label{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}
666	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart}
667	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke (Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing).}
668	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying the codim-1 hypotheses of
669	  \ref{def:codim1_cycles}. For a Weil divisor
670	  \(D = \sum_Y n_Y \cdot Y \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\), the \emph{positive part}
671	  \((D)_0 \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is the Weil divisor
672	  \[
673	    (D)_0 \;:=\; \sum_{Y \,:\, n_Y > 0} n_Y \cdot Y.
674	  \]
675	  Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
676	  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
677	  presentation, the positive part is obtained either by the pointwise lattice
678	  join with the zero divisor — \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\) — or, equivalently
679	  and more transparently for typeclass synthesis, by mapping the coefficients
680	  through the zero-preserving function \(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0\):
681	  \(\,(D)_0 = \mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\;(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0)\;D\). The two
682	  forms agree by the universal property of \(\mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\). The
683	  Lean implementation uses the \(\mathtt{mapRange}\) form to avoid the
684	  noncomputable \(\Finsupp.\mathtt{semilatticeSup}\) synthesis. The complementary \emph{negative part}
685	  \((D)_\infty\) is defined by \((D)_\infty := (-D)_0\), so that
686	  \(D = (D)_0 - (D)_\infty\) is the canonical decomposition into an
687	  effective (non-negative-coefficient) divisor minus an effective divisor.
688	  On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\), where the prime divisors
689	  are the closed points and the coefficients are integers, the positive part
690	  reduces to keeping only the closed points whose multiplicity is positive
691	  — i.e. precisely the zeros of any rational function whose principal
692	  divisor is \(D\), in the case \(D = \mathrm{div}(f)\).
693	\end{definition}
694	\begin{lemma}
695	
696	\leanok
697	[Degree of the positive part of \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) for a non-constant rational
698	function equals the function-field extension degree]
699	  \label{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
701	  % NOTE (iter-194 refactor v2 `lane-i-localparameter-signature-v2`):
702	  % the iter-193 `hlp`-augmented signature was diagnosed STILL false by
703	  % the Lane I prover via counter-witness `K = K(C) = \bar k(u),
704	  % t = u(u-1)`. Iter-194 plan-phase refactor reshapes the Lean
705	  % signature per Option (b): drop the abstract `K` parameter and pin
706	  % `t \in K(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = (\text{ProjectiveLineBar
707	  % kbar}).left.functionField`; replace `hlp` with the stronger
708	  % uniformiser hypothesis
709	  %   `hLPUnif : \exists Y_0 \in \text{PrimeDivisor}(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}),
710	  %     order(Y_0, t) = 1 \land
711	  %     \forall Y, order(Y, t) > 0 \implies Y = Y_0`,
712	  % i.e. \(t\) has a unique simple zero on \(\mathbb P^1\). This rules
713	  % out the iter-193 counter-witness because (i) \(t = u(u-1)\) lives
714	  % in \(K(\mathbb P^1)\) but has TWO simple zeros (\(\{u = 0\}\),
715	  % \(\{u = 1\}\)), so the uniqueness clause fails; (ii) any
716	  % function-field embedding `K = K(C)` is now disallowed at the type
717	  % level. The consumer
718	  % `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`
719	  % (`RationalCurveIso.lean`) supplies the witness via
720	  % `localParameterAtInfty kbar = 1/u` (a Möbius transformation; simple
721	  % zero only at \(\infty\)) as a typed sorry owed iter-194+. Refactor
722	  % v2 net cost: \(+2\) typed-sorry typeclass instances on
723	  % `ProjectiveLineBar` (\verb|IsLocallyNoetherian|,
724	  % \verb|IsRegularInCodimensionOne|; both directly derivable from
725	  % `SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1` ⟹ regular ⟹ Noetherian + DVR-stalks).
726	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137 (multiplicativity
727	  % of degree under finite pullback) (read from
728	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
729	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (pullback of divisors under finite
730	  % morphisms), tag 0BE6 (degree under finite morphism).
731	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.9. \emph{Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a finite
732	  % morphism of nonsingular curves. Then for any divisor \(D\) on \(Y\),
733	  % \(\deg(f^\ast D) = [K(X) : K(Y)] \cdot \deg D\).}"
734	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137.}
735	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over an
736	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
737	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism.
738	  Let \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) be a local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)
739	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
740	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)). Write \(f := \varphi^{\#} t_\infty
741	  \in K(C)\) for the pullback of \(t_\infty\) along \(\varphi\). Then \(f\) is
742	  nonzero (because \(\varphi\) is non-constant; equivalently \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
743	  is a proper closed subscheme of \(C\)) and the degree of the positive
744	  part of the principal divisor of \(f\) equals the function-field
745	  extension degree:
746	  \[
747	    \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}(f))_0\bigr)
748	    \;=\; \bigl[\,K(C) \,:\, \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,\bigr]
749	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
750	  \]
751	  Equivalently, \((\mathrm{div}(f))_0 = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\) (the effective
752	  preimage divisor of \(\infty\)) and \(\deg(\varphi^{*}[\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)\).
753	\end{lemma}
754	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
755	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
756	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
757	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
758	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
759	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)
760	% gives \(\deg(f^\ast D) = \sum_{P} n_P \sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q
761	% = \sum_P n_P [K(X) : K(Y)] = [K(X) : K(Y)] \deg(D)\)."
762	\begin{proof}
763	  Following Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9 specialised to the divisor
764	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
765	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
766	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
767	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
768	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
769	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
770	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
771	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
772	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
773	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
774	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
775	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
776	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
777	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of
778	  \(A\); both are Dedekind domains. Apply
779	  \texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia} (Mathlib,
780	  \texttt{Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic}) to the
781	  ring-theoretic extension \(A \to B\) at the maximal ideal
782	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\):
783	  \[
784	    \sum_{Q \,\colon\, \mathfrak m_\infty \subset Q} e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty} \cdot f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}
785	    \;=\; \dim_{A / \mathfrak m_\infty}\!\bigl(B / \mathfrak m_\infty B\bigr)
786	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) \;=\; d,
787	  \]
788	  where the second equality uses
789	  \texttt{Ideal.finrank\_quotient\_map} (which identifies the residue-field
790	  quotient with the function-field extension degree for a Dedekind extension
791	  at a maximal ideal). The closed points \(Q \subset \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) above
792	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\) are precisely the closed points
793	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty) \subset C\); the ramification index
794	  \(e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is the order \(\ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\)
795	  of the pulled-back local parameter at \(P\); the residue degree
796	  \(f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is \([\kappa(P) : \bar k] = 1\) (every closed
797	  point of \(C\) over \(\bar k\) has residue field \(\bar k\)). Therefore
798	  \[
799	    \sum_{P \,\in\, \varphi^{-1}(\infty)} \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \;=\; d
800	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
801	  \]
802	  The left-hand side equals the degree of the positive part of
803	  \(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\), because: at every closed point
804	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the order is \(\geq 1\) (pullback of a local
805	  parameter under a finite morphism is itself a uniformiser-power, hence
806	  has positive order at every preimage); at every closed point
807	  \(P \in C \setminus \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the rational function
808	  \(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty\) is regular and nonzero (because \(t_\infty\) is
809	  regular and nonzero on \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)), hence the
810	  order at such a \(P\) is \(\geq 0\); the points where the order is
811	  \emph{strictly} positive are exactly the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\).
812	  Therefore
813	  \(\,(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0 = \sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
814	  \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \cdot [P] = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\,\), and
815	  taking degree completes the proof.
816	\end{proof}
817	\section{Divisor class group}
818	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
819	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
820	the \emph{Picard group} of \(C\) in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
821	interpretation \(\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)\) requires the construction of
822	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\), which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
823	out of scope here.
824	\begin{definition}
825	\leanok
826	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
827	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
828	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
829	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
830	  % and of \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)) (read from
831	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
832	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). Two divisors \(D\)
833	  % and \(D'\) are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\),
834	  % if \(D - D'\) is a principal divisor. The group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) of
835	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
836	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\), and is denoted by
837	  % \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)."
838	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
839	  \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)).}
840	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). Two Weil divisors \(D, D' \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) are
841	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\), if and only if there
842	  exists \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\) with
843	  \[
844	    D - D' \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X).
845	  \]
846	  The relation \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (the
847	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
848	  identities \(\mathrm{div}(1) = 0\), \(\mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) = -\mathrm{div}(f)\), $\mathrm{div}(fg) =
849	  \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g)$ via \ref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
850	  quotient group \(\Cl(X) := \mathrm{Div}(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)\) is the
851	  \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\). On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over
852	  \(\bar k\), \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
853	  \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) descends to a homomorphism
854	  \(\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z\).
855	\end{definition}
856	\section{Out of scope}
857	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
858	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
859	\begin{itemize}
860	  \item The line-bundle construction \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to a Weil
861	    divisor \(D\), the calculation of its global sections, and the
862	    identification \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
863	    \(D \sim D'\), lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
864	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
865	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
866	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) and its genus-\(0\) specialisation
867	    \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) live in \texttt{RR.2}
868	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
869	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
870	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)''
871	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
872	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
873	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
874	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) together
875	    with its degree-\(1\) identification.
876	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
877	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
878	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
879	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
880	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
881	    \(\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)\)) are out of scope: this chapter is
882	    strictly curve-focused.
883	  \item The proof of \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
884	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
885	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
886	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
887	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
888	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
889	\end{itemize}

24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
166	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
167	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by
170	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
174	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
175	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[
178	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
179	    \qquad
180	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
181	  \]
182	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
183	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
184	  the pullback maps \(g^\sharp\) of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}
189	
190	\begin{lemma}\leanok
191	[Naturality respects the group structure]
192	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
196	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
197	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
198	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
199	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
200	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
202	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
203	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
204	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
205	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
206	  %  [\dots]
207	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
208	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
209	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
210	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
211	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
212	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
213	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
214	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
215	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
216	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
217	  composition laws \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
218	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp\) hold as identities of group
219	  homomorphisms.
220	\end{lemma}
221	
222	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
223	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
224	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
225	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
226	\begin{proof}
227	  
228	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
229	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
230	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
231	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
232	  structure sheaf, hence the map
233	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
234	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
235	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
236	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
237	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
238	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
239	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
240	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	\begin{theorem}\leanok
244	[\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is a group-valued presheaf]
245	  \label{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
247	  
248	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
249	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (relative Picard functor as a functor into
250	  % abelian groups)
251	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
252	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
253	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
254	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
255	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
256	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
257	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
258	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.}
259	  The assignment \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp},
260	  together with the structure maps \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) of
261	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, defines a presheaf of abelian groups
262	  \[
263	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\;
264	                                     \AddCommGroup
265	  \]
266	  on the category of \(k\)-schemes.
267	\end{theorem}
268	
269	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with the prior theorems, Kleiman \S 2 does not supply
270	% a separate proof body --- the presheaf-of-abelian-groups property is part of
271	% the definition df:aPf + df:Pfs. The project-side proof assembles the lemmas
272	% above; no external proof is being translated.
273	\begin{proof}
274	  
275	  The object map is well defined by
276	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}: for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the value
277	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is an abelian group. The morphism map
278	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism by
279	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, and satisfies the identity and
280	  composition laws by the same lemma. These data assemble into a functor
281	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\), i.e.\ a presheaf of abelian groups on
282	  \((\Sch/k)\).
283	\end{proof}

285	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
286	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
287	
288	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
289	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
290	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
291	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
292	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
293	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
294	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
295	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
296	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
297	
298	\begin{definition}\leanok
299	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
300	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
302	  % NOTE (iter-176 review): the iter-176 Lane G prover renamed this declaration
303	  % from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`` to
304	  % ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf`` to avoid a name collision with
305	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
306	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
307	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
308	  % below is also corrected.
309	  
310	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
311	  % Definition df:Pfs --- the second half of df:Pfs names the \'etale sheaf
312	  % associated to the relative Picard functor
313	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318
314	  %  and the surrounding paragraph L1320-L1328).
315	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
316	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
317	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
318	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
319	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
320	  % topologies  by
321	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
322	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
323	  %  \end{dfn}
324	  %  [\dots]
325	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
326	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
327	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
328	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
329	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
330	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
331	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
332	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
333	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
334	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
335	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
336	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
337	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
338	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):
339	  \[
340	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;
341	       \bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\bigr)^{\sim_{\et}}.
342	  \]
343	  Equivalently, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is the unique (up to canonical
344	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)
345	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
346	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
347	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
348	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
349	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
350	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
351	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
352	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
353	\end{definition}
354	
355	\begin{theorem}[\'Etale sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure]
356	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
359	  % Def.~df:Pfs --- the \'etale-sheafified functor \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named
360	  % alongside the abelian-group-valued \(\Pic_{X/S}\) in the same definition,
361	  % i.e.\ the sheafification lives in the same target category
362	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
363	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
364	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
365	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
366	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
367	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
368	  % topologies  by
369	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
370	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
371	  %  \end{dfn}"
372	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
373	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named together with the
374	  abelian-group-valued relative Picard functor, meaning the sheaf is taken in
375	  the category of abelian groups).}
376	  The \'etale sheafification
377	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification} is a
378	  sheaf with values in the category of abelian groups: the canonical morphism
379	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
380	  presheaves, and for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) the value
381	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is naturally an abelian group whose structure
382	  maps make \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) a presheaf
383	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\).
384	\end{theorem}
385	
386	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a self-contained proof of
387	% this auxiliary statement --- it is the standard fact that the sheafification
388	% of a presheaf of abelian groups in a Grothendieck topology is again a
389	% presheaf of abelian groups (equivalently, the forgetful functor
390	% \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves filtered colimits, and the
391	% plus-construction is built from such colimits). The Lean side appeals to
392	% Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) machinery.
393	\begin{proof}
394	  
395	  The \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck topology, and the
396	  forgetful functor \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves and reflects the
397	  filtered colimits used by the plus-construction. Therefore the
398	  sheafification of a presheaf \(\mathcal{F} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\)
399	  in the underlying \'etale site agrees, after the forgetful functor to sets,
400	  with the sheafification of the underlying set-valued presheaf
401	  \(U \circ \mathcal{F}\); equivalently, the sheaf
402	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} = (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) is canonically a
403	  sheaf of abelian groups, the canonical morphism
404	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
405	  presheaves, and the restriction maps are group homomorphisms. This is a
406	  standard consequence of the plus-construction with values in an algebraic
407	  category; on the Lean side it is the Mathlib pattern
408	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) applied with target category
409	  \(\AddCommGroup\).
410	\end{proof}

413	\label{sec:relpic_lean_encoding}
414
