43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
190	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
191	
192	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
193	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
194	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
195	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
196	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
197	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
198	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
199	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
200	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
201	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
202	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
203	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
204	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
205	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
206	Mathlib gap, see the \texttt{\% NOTE (iter-194)} block on
207	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} just below.
208	
209	\begin{lemma}
210	
211	\leanok
212	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
213	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
214	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
215	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
216	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
217	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
218	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
219	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
220	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
221	  \(S_M\)-module.
222	\end{lemma}
223	
224	\begin{proof}
225	  The canonical comparison map
226	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
227	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
228	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
229	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
230	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
231	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
232	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
233	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
234	  the conclusion.
235	\end{proof}
236	
237	\begin{lemma}
238	
239	\leanok
240	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
241	  the relative dimension]
242	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
244	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
245	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
246	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
247	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
248	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
249	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
250	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
251	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
252	\end{lemma}
253	
254	\begin{proof}
255	  Mathlib's
256	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
257	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
258	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
259	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
260	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
261	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
262	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
264	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
265	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
266	  ranks equal \(n\), so
267	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
268	\end{proof}

413	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
414	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
415	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
416	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
417	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
418	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
419	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
420	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
421	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).

437	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
438	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
439	
440	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
441	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
442	point.
443	
444	\begin{theorem}
445	
446	\leanok
447	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
448	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
449	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
450	  
451	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
452	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
453	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
454	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
455	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
456	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
457	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
458	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
459	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
460	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
461	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
462	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
463	
464	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
465	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
466	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
467	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
468	  in \(X\).
469	\end{theorem}
470	
471	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
472	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
473	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
474	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
475	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
476	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
477	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
478	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
479	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
480	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
481	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
482	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
483	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
484	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
485	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
486	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
487	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
488	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
489	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
490	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
491	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
492	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
493	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
494	% which is a contradiction."
495	\begin{proof}
496	  
497	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
498	
499	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
500	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
501	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
502	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
503	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
504	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
505	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
506	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
507	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
508	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
509	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
510	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
511	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
512	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
513	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
514	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
515	
516	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
517	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
518	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
519	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
520	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
521	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
522	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
523	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
524	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
525	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
526	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
527	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
528	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
529	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
530	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
531	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
532	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
533	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
534	
535	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
536	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
537	  agree on all of \(X\).
538	\end{proof}
539	
540	\begin{remark}
541	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
542	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
543	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
544	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
545	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
546	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
547	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
548	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
549	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
550	
551	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
552	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
553	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
554	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
555	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
556	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
557	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
558	\end{remark}
559	
560	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
561	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
562	
563	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
564	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
565	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
566	this forces the locus to be empty.
567	
568	\begin{lemma}
569	
570	\leanok
571	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
572	  variety]
573	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
574	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
575	  
576	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
577	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
578	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
579	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
580	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
581	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
582	  % divisors).}"
583	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
584	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
585	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
586	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
587	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
588	  union of prime divisors).
589	
590	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
591	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
592	\end{lemma}

618	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
619	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
620	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
621	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
622	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
623	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
624	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
625	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
626	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
627	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
628	\begin{proof}
629	  
630	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
631	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
632	
633	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
634	  \[
635	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
636	    \qquad
637	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
638	  \]
639	  via the composition
640	  \[
641	    U \times U
642	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
643	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
644	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
645	  \]
646	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
647	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
648	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
649	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
650	
651	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
652	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
653	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
654	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
655	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
656	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
657	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
658	  identity
659	  \[
660	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
661	    \qquad u \in W',
662	  \]
663	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
664	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
665	
666	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
667	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
668	  \(K(X \times X)\):
669	  \[
670	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
671	  \]
672	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
673	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
674	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
675	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
676	
677	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
678	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
679	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
680	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
681	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
682	  \[
683	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
684	  \]
685	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
686	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
687	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
688	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
689	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
690	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
691	  \[
692	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
693	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
694	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
695	  \]
696	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
697	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
698	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
699	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
700	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
701	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
702	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
703	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
704	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
705	
706	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
707	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
708	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
709	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
710	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
711	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
712	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
713	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
714	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
715	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
716	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
717	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
718	\end{proof}
719	
720	\begin{remark}
721	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
722	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
723	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
724	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
725	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
726	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
727	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
728	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
729	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
730	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
731	\end{remark}
732	
733	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
734	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
735	
736	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
737	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
738	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
739	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
740	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
741	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
742	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
743	
744	\begin{theorem}
745	
746	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
747	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
748	  
749	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
750	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
751	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
752	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
753	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
754	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
755	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
756	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
757	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
758	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
759	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
760	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
761	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
762	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
763	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
764	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
765	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
766	  % NOTE (iter-194 writer): no corresponding Lean declaration found in
767	  % CodimOneExtension.lean --- iter-195 must either rename to match an
768	  % existing decl or add a new Lean stub. (The iter-179 detach was
769	  % structural, not a \lean{...} typo: the substantive Hartshorne-II.6
770	  % ord_W >= 0 reformulation requires the
771	  % Scheme.RationalMap-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
772	  % not in Mathlib at commit b80f227 and is not yet built in this
773	  % project. The weaker iter-179 reshuffle Lean declaration
774	  % mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point is blueprint-pinned
775	  % at \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} as the honest
776	  % fallback; thm:weil_divisor_obstruction here remains unpinned at the
777	  % \lean{...} level until the genuine pullback decl lands.)
778	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
779	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
780	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
781	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
782	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
783	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
784	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
785	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
786	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
787	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
788	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
789	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
790	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
791	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
792	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
793	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
794	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
795	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
796	  \begin{enumerate}
797	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
798	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
799	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
800	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
801	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
802	      satisfying
803	      \[
804	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
805	      \]
806	  \end{enumerate}
807	
808	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
809	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
810	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
811	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
812	\end{theorem}
813	
814	\begin{proof}
815	  
816	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
817	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
818	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
819	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
820	  \[
821	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
822	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
823	  \]
824	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
825	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
826	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
827	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
828	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
829	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
830	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
831	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
832	
833	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
834	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
835	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
836	\end{proof}
837	
838	\begin{remark}
839	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
840	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
841	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
842	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
843	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
844	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
845	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
846	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
847	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
848	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
849	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
850	\end{remark}
851	
852	\begin{lemma}
853	
854	\leanok
855	  [Definedness at a prime divisor, repackaged as a through-the-point partial map]
856	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
857	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
858	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
859	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
860	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
861	  following are equivalent:
862	  \begin{enumerate}
863	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
864	      \(W\)).
865	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
866	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
867	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
868	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
869	  \end{enumerate}
870	\end{lemma}
871	
872	\begin{proof}
873	
874	  The lemma is a definitional reshuffle of the Mathlib membership predicate
875	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain}, which already states that
876	  \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) iff there is some partial-map representative of
877	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
878	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
879	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
880	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
881	  the unfolded definition.
882	\end{proof}
883	
884	\begin{remark}
885	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}
886	  \textbf{Relationship to \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.}
887	  \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} is the iter-179 Lane~D honest
888	  fallback for the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole''
889	  reformulation that \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full
890	  \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form. The reshuffle captures only the
891	  definitional half of the equivalence (existence of a partial-map
892	  representative through a prime-divisor generic point); upgrading it to the
893	  substantive \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form requires the
894	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap}-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
895	  not shipped by Mathlib at commit \texttt{b80f227}. See the iter-179
896	  \texttt{\% NOTE} block on \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} for the
897	  history.
898	\end{remark}

4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the

32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The
126	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} dependency is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
161	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
4	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.
5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
15	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
18	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
20	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
21	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
22	  % such \(b\) arises from a unique map \(a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}\) such that
23	  % \(a(0)=0\).  Since \(B^*\) is integral, \(a\) factors through \(P\).  Thus the
24	  % maps \(a\:B^*\to P\) and \(b\:X\to B\) are in bijective correspondence.
25	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps \(b'\:B\to B'\) such
26	  % that \(b'(0)=0\).  In particular, \(1_P\) corresponds to a natural map
27	  % \(u\:X\to A\) such that \(u(x)=0\), and every map \(b\:X\to B\) factors
28	  % uniquely through \(u\)."
29	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
30	  Let \((C, P)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with a marked \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\). A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) is an \emph{Albanese object} for \((C, P)\) if there exists a morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) sending \(P\) to the identity element \(\eta \in J\), and such that every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) factors uniquely as \(f = \iota \circ g\) for some morphism of group schemes \(g \colon J \to A\).
31	\end{definition}
32	\begin{remark}
33	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
34	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on \(J\) --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on \(\iota\) and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on \(J\) sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on \(\Jac(C)\) before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety \(A\) in the universal-property clause.
35	\end{remark}
36	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
37	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term \(h\) packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition \(P \circ \iota = \eta_J\) and the universal-factorisation property of \(\iota\). The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
38	\begin{definition}
39	\leanok
40	[The Albanese universal morphism]
41	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
42	  
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
44	  Given a pointed curve \((C, P)\) and an Albanese term \(h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\), the \emph{universal morphism} of \(h\) is the morphism
45	  \[
46	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
47	  \]
48	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of \(h\).
49	\end{definition}
50	\begin{lemma}
51	\leanok
52	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
53	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
54	  
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
56	  The universal morphism \(h.\mathtt{ofCurve}\) sends the marked point \(P\) to the identity of \(J\):
57	  \[
58	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
59	  \]
60	\end{lemma}
61	\begin{lemma}
62	\leanok
63	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
64	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
65	  
66	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
67	  For every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), there exists a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g\).
68	\end{lemma}
69	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point \(P \in C(k)\) as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that \(P\), and applies the corresponding helper above.
70	\begin{theorem}\leanok
71	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
72	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
74	  Let \(J_1, J_2\) be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve \((C, P)\), with universal pointed morphisms \(\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1\) and \(\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2\) (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism \(e \colon J_1 \to J_2\) satisfying \(\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e\). The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation \(\mathrm{id}\), and the produced morphism \(e\) is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of \(e\) as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{remark}
77	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
78	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism \(h \colon J_2 \to J_1\) and verifies \(g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}\) and \(h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}\) (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple \((g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})\). The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
79	  \[
80	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
81	  \]
82	  The natural strengthening would be to return
83	  \[
84	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
85	  \]
86	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
87	\end{remark}
88	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
89	\begin{definition}\leanok
90	[Jacobian]
91	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
92	  \label{def:Jacobian}
93	  
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
95	  The \emph{Jacobian} of \(C\) is the abelian variety \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) obtained as the Albanese object of \(C\): a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g(C)\) equipped with a universal morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\). Formally, \(\Jac(C)\) is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-\(P\)) Albanese witness for \(C\) provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
96	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the Jacobian collapses to \(\Spec k\) itself, which is the terminal object in the category of \(k\)-schemes. When \(g(C) > 0\) the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers \(C\) itself (after choosing a \(k\)-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: \(\Jac(C)\) is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-\(0\) specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over \(k\) of relative dimension \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\Spec k\).
97	\end{definition}
98	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
99	The four protected instances on \(\Jac(C)\) are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
100	\begin{theorem}\leanok
101	[Group object structure]
102	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
103	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
104	  \(\Jac(C)\) carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
105	\end{theorem}
106	\begin{proof}
107	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, \(\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J\) as a \(k\)-scheme, where \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\) is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field \(\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J\), so \(\Jac(C)\) inherits it by projection.
108	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if \(\iota \colon C \to J\) is the universal morphism sending the marked point \(P\) to the identity, then the diagonal \(C \times C \to J \times J\), post-composed with the universal morphism out of \(C \times C\) (factored through \(J\) by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
109	\end{proof}
110	\begin{theorem}\leanok
111	[Smoothness and dimension]
112	  \label{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus}
114	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Cor.~cor:sm (smoothness/dimension over a field) + Cor.~cor:ch0 (char-\(0\) smoothness) + Prp.~prp:P0 (smooth over reduced \(S\)); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~34, 38  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)

117	  % \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\) Equality holds if and
118	  % only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
119	  % smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere."
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cor:ch0): "Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).
121	  % Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is
122	  % of characteristic \(0\), then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension
123	  % \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere."
124	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:P0, smoothness clause): "Assume \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists,
125	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}\).  For \(s\in S\), assume all the
126	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are smooth of the same dimension.  Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\)
127	  % has an open group subscheme \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of finite type whose fibers
128	  % are the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\).  Furthermore, if \(S\) is reduced, then
129	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) is smooth over \(S\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Cor.~(cor:sm), Cor.~(cor:ch0), Prp.~(prp:P0).}
131	  \(\Jac(C) \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\).
132	\end{theorem}
133	\begin{proof}
134	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\); the relative dimension \(g(C)\) is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
135	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of \(C\) equals the genus \(g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to \(\Jac(C)\) at the identity is canonically identified with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of \(\mathcal O_C\) is classified by an element of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\), and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\). Smoothness of \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any \(k\)-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of \(\Jac(C)\) at every \(k\)-rational point is the same as at the identity.
136	\end{proof}

138	[Properness]
139	  \label{thm:Jacobian_proper}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instIsProper}

142	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, and represents
143	  % \(\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}\).  For \(s\in S\), assume all the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are
144	  % smooth of the same dimension.  Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) has an open group
145	  % subscheme \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of finite type whose fibers are the
146	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\).  Furthermore, if \(S\) is reduced, then \(\IPicz_{X/S}\)
147	  % is smooth over \(S\).  Moreover, if all the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are complete
148	  % and if \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is separated over \(S\), then \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) is closed
149	  % in \(\IPic_{X/S}\) and proper over \(S\)."

152	\end{theorem}
153	\begin{proof}
154	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{proper} : \mathrm{IsProper}\, J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness.
155	  Mathematically, properness of the Albanese variety is part of its construction. In the Picard-scheme route, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a closed subgroup scheme of the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which is itself proper over \(\Spec k\) because \(C\) is proper and geometrically connected (FGA representability for \(\Pic\) of a smooth proper geometrically connected curve produces a proper group scheme; cf.\ FGA Explained, Chapter~9). In the symmetric-power route, properness of \(\Pic^g_{C/k}\) (and hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) after translation) follows from properness of \(\Sym^g(C)\) together with the universal closedness of the surjection \(\sigma\). In either case, properness descends to \(\Jac(C)\).
156	\end{proof}

158	[Geometric irreducibility]
159	  \label{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}

164	  % identity element \(e\).  \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.  \tu{(2)} Then \(G\)
165	  % is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open subscheme.  \tu{(3)}
166	  % Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it is
167	  % geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending \(k\)."
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:pic0): "Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).

172	  % identity \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
