408	\begin{lemma}
409	[Assembly of the strata by Noetherian induction]
410	  \label{lem:noetherian_induction_strata}
411	  
412	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Assembly via
413	  % direct images \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\) (read from
414	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1992-L2090).
415	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
416	  % "Let \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\) denote the projection. Consider the
417	  %  coherent sheaves \(E_0, \ldots, E_n\) on \(S\), defined by
418	  %  \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\mbox{ for } i=0,\ldots,n.\)
419	  %  By applying the special case of of the theorem (where the relative dimension
420	  %  \(n\) of \(\PP^n_S\) is \(0\)) to the sheaf \(E_0\) on \(\PP^0_S=S\), we get
421	  %  a stratification \((W_{e_0})\) of \(S\)
422	  %  indexed by integers \(e_0\), such that for any morphism
423	  %  \(f: T\to S\) the pull-back \(f^*E_0\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-module
424	  %  of rank \(e_0\) if and only if \(f\) factors via \(W_{e_0} \hra S\).
425	  %  Next, for each stratum \(W_{e_0}\), we take the
426	  %  flattening stratification \((W_{e_0,e_1})\) for \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\), and so on.
427	  %  Thus in \(n+1\) steps, we obtain finitely many
428	  %  locally closed subschemes
429	  %  \(W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \subset S\)
430	  %  such that for any morphism \(f:T\to S\) the pull-back
431	  %  \(f^*E_i\) for \(i=0,\ldots,n\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-modules of
432	  %  of constant rank \(e_i\) if and only if \(f\) factors
433	  %  via \(W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \hra S\). [...]
434	  %  This shows that at any point \(s\in W_f\), the Hilbert polynomial
435	  %  \(P_s(m)\) equals \(f\). The desired locally closed subscheme \(S_f \subset S\),
436	  %  whose existence is asserted by the theorem,
437	  %  will turn out to be a certain closed subscheme \(S_f \subset W_f\) whose
438	  %  underlying subset is all of \(|W_f|\). [...]
439	  %  As \(S_f\) is the flattening stratification for \(\pi_*\F(p)\),
440	  %  the property (iii) of the theorem follows from the corresponding property in
441	  %  the case \(n=0\), applied to the sheaf \(\pi_*\F(p)\) on \(S\)."
442	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (assembly step combining the \(n=0\) special case
443	  applied to direct images \(\pi_*\F(N+i)\)).}
444	  Let \(S\), \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\), \(\F\) be as in
445	  Theorem~REF.  There exists an integer
446	  \(N \ge 0\) such that for every \(s \in S\) the higher cohomologies
447	  \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) vanish for all \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N\), and the base-change
448	  homomorphism \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism
449	  for \(m \ge N\), \(s \in S\).  Iterating the \(n=0\) stratification
450	  (Lemma~REF) on the coherent sheaves \(E_i := \pi_* \F(N+i)\)
451	  for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) produces a finite collection of locally-closed
452	  subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\) indexed by tuples
453	  \((e_0, \dots, e_n) \in \Z^{n+1}\). Re-indexing each tuple by the unique
454	  numerical polynomial \(f \in \Q[\lambda]\) of degree \(\le n\) with
455	  \(f(N+i) = e_i\) gives a finite stratification \(\{W_f\}\) of \(S\), and the
456	  locally-closed subschemes \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) of
457	  Theorem~REF are obtained by further
458	  intersecting with the closed-subscheme conditions that
459	  \(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) be locally free of rank \(f(N+i)\) for all \(i \ge 0\)
460	  (terminating by Noetherianity).
461	\end{lemma}
462	
463	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: full proof is the long body of [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem
464	% ``flattening stratification'', spanning L1844-L2090 (~250 lines: special
465	% case, Noetherian induction, statements (A)/(B)/(C), then the assembly via
466	% direct images and the closed-subscheme construction inside each \(W_f\)).
467	% Verbatim retrieved per-sub-lemma above; the structural restatement below in
468	% project notation is the canonical Nitsure-style restated proof.
469	\begin{proof}
470	  
471	  By Lemma~REF, the family of Hilbert polynomials
472	  \(\{P_s\}\) on \(S\) is finite, and there is a finite stratification
473	  \(\{V_i\}\) of \(S\) on each of which \(\F\) is flat with locally-constant
474	  Hilbert polynomial.  Apply semi-continuity (cohomology and base change,
475	  [Hartshorne], III.12 / [Stacks Project], tag 02KH for the \(H^0\)-version) on
476	  the noetherian \(V_i\) to obtain a uniform integer \(N_1\) with
477	  \(R^r \pi_* \F(m) = 0\) for \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\), and \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m)) = 0\)
478	  for all \(s \in S\), \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\) (statement (B) of Nitsure's proof).
479	  Choose \(r_i \ge N_1\) on each \(V_i\) such that the base-change comparison
480	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_{V_i} \to (\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m)\) is an isomorphism for
481	  \(m \ge r_i\); composing with the cohomology-and-base-change isomorphism
482	  \(((\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m))|_s \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) for
483	  the flat family on \(V_i\) yields uniform \(N = \max r_i\) such that
484	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism for \(m \ge N\)
485	  and all \(s \in S\) (statement (C)).
486	
487	  Apply Lemma~REF sequentially to the coherent sheaves
488	  \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\): \(E_0\) produces a
489	  stratification \(\{W_{e_0}\}\) of \(S\); on each \(W_{e_0}\) apply the lemma to
490	  \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\) to refine to \(\{W_{e_0, e_1}\}\), and continue. After \(n+1\)
491	  steps the \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n}\) form a finite locally-closed stratification
492	  of \(S\) universal for the property ``each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free
493	  \(\OO_T\)-module of constant rank \(e_i\)''.  Re-index \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
494	  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\) to get
495	  the family \(\{W_f\}\) indexed by polynomials.  At any \(s \in W_f\), statement
496	  (C) and vanishing (B) force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has degree \(\le n\) and is
497	  determined by its values at \(N, N+1, \dots, N+n\)).
498	
499	  The Hilbert-polynomial-coincidence locus \(|W_f|\) may have non-flat scheme
500	  structure; refine to \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) by intersecting with the
501	  closed-subscheme conditions ``\(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) is locally-free of rank
502	  \(f(N+i)\)'' for all \(i \ge 0\).  These conditions are cut out by an increasing
503	  chain of coherent ideal sheaves \(I_0 \subseteq I_0 + I_1 \subseteq \cdots\) on
504	  \(W_f\); Noetherianity terminates the chain in finitely many steps, defining a
505	  coherent ideal sheaf \(I\) and a closed subscheme \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) with
506	  \(|S_f| = |W_f|\) on which \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) is locally-free of rank \(f(N+i)\)
507	  for all \(i \ge 0\). The base-change-without-flatness lemma (cf.\ [Nitsure],
508	  \S3) yields some \(N' \ge N\) such that for \(i \ge N'\),
509	  \((\pi_* \F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism, hence
510	  the graded \(A\)-module \(\bigoplus_{i \ge N'} (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is
511	  locally-free on \(S_f\), hence \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\). The
512	  closure-of-strata property (iii) reduces to the corresponding property in
513	  the \(n=0\) case for \(\pi_* \F(p)\) on \(S\) at any \(p \ge N\) separating all
514	  Hilbert polynomials.
515	\end{proof}

517	\section{Universal property of the strata}
518	\label{sec:flatstrat_universal}
519	
520	\begin{theorem}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Universal property of the flattening stratification]
524	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_universal}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal}
526	  
527	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', part (ii)
528	  % universal property
529	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1826-L1834).
530	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
531	  % "\textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
532	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
533	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
534	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
535	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
536	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
537	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
538	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
539	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\)."
540	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (part (ii) of the main theorem); cf.\ [Stacks
541	  Project], tag 052H.}
542	  Let \(S\), \(\F\), and \(\{S_f\}_{f \in I}\) be as in
543	  Theorem~REF.  Set
544	  \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) with the coproduct morphism
545	  \(i : S' \to S\) assembled from the locally-closed inclusions.  Then \(i^* \F\)
546	  is flat over \(S'\), and the pair \((S', i)\) is universal: for any morphism
547	  \(\varphi : T \to S\) of noetherian schemes (one may take \(T\) arbitrary, the
548	  Noetherian hypothesis on \(S\) being preserved), the pullback
549	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is \(T\)-flat if and only if \(\varphi\) factors
550	  uniquely through \(i\). In particular each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
551	\end{theorem}
552	
553	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that the morphism \(\coprod_f S_f \,\to \,S\)
554	% indeed has the property (ii) of the theorem.
555	% By Lemma REF,
556	% there exists some \(N' \ge N\) such that for all \(i\ge N'\),
557	% the base-change
558	% \((\pi_*\F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_*\F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism for
559	% each \(S_f\). Therefore
560	% \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\) by Lemma REF,
561	% as the direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N'\) are locally
562	% free over \(S_f\). Conversely, if \(\phi : T\to S\) is a morphism such
563	% that \(\F_T\) is flat, then the Hilbert polynomial is locally
564	% constant over \(T\). Let \(T_f\) be the open and closed subscheme of \(T\)
565	% where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\). Clearly, the set
566	% map \(|T_f| \to |S|\) factors via \(|S_f|\). But as the direct images
567	% \({\pi_T}_*\F_T(i)\) are locally free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\), it
568	% follows in fact that the schematic morphism \(T_f \to S\) factors
569	% via \(S_f\), proving the property (ii) of the theorem."
570	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2065-L2082.)
571	\begin{proof}
572	  
573	  Flatness of \(i^* \F\) over \(S'\) was established at the end of the proof of
574	  Theorem~REF (the
575	  base-change-without-flatness lemma plus the graded-module-flatness criterion
576	  applied to the coherent sheaves \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) on each \(S_f\)).
577	  Conversely, if \(\varphi : T \to S\) satisfies \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is
578	  \(T\)-flat, then \(T\)-flatness of the family forces the Hilbert polynomial to be
579	  locally constant on \(T\).  Decompose \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed
580	  subschemes where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\).  The set map \(|T_f| \to |S|\)
581	  factors through \(|S_f|\); further, since the direct images
582	  \((\pi_T)_* \varphi^* \F(i)\) are locally-free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\) for $i
583	  \ge N'\(, the universal property of \)S_f \subseteq W_f$ as the closed
584	  subscheme cut out by these local-freeness conditions
585	  (Lemma~REF) yields a unique scheme
586	  morphism \(T_f \to S_f\) extending the set map. Assembling over \(f\) gives the
587	  unique factorisation \(\varphi = i \circ \tilde \varphi\).
588	\end{proof}

590	\section{Application: relative curves}
591	\label{sec:flatstrat_curve_application}
592	
593	In the Route~A consumer setting, the input to the flattening-stratification
594	machinery is the pullback \(C \times_k T \to T\) where \(C\) is a smooth proper
595	curve over a field \(k\) and \(T\) is any \(k\)-scheme.  The hypotheses of
596	Nitsure's Theorem~REF demand
597	\emph{projective} relative dimension, not merely \emph{proper}; we record that
598	no extra work is required to instantiate the theorem in our setting.
599	
600	\begin{lemma}
601	[Smooth proper curve over \(k\) is projective]
602	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_curve_projective}
603	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6 (curve \(\Rightarrow\) quasi-projective;
604	  % proper \(\Rightarrow\) projective for curves), and standard consequences in
605	  % chapter III. The precise statement ``every smooth proper curve over a field
606	  % is projective'' is one of the classical equivalences for \(\dim 1\) and is
607	  % textbook material; this block is project-bespoke (no verbatim source
608	  % quote is reproduced here as the result is folklore-level and cited only
609	  % structurally for the Route~A reduction).
610	  \textit{Source: classical; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.6.7 \& II.7.6 (curves).}
611	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\).  Then \(C \to \Spec k\) is
612	  projective.  Consequently, for any noetherian \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the relative
613	  curve \(C \times_k T \to T\) is projective in the sense of Grothendieck (there
614	  exists a relatively very ample line bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\)).
615	\end{lemma}
616	
617	\begin{proof}
618	  Projectivity of a smooth proper curve over a field is classical
619	  ([Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6; alternatively: every divisor of large
620	  positive degree on a smooth proper curve \(C\) is very ample, providing the
621	  embedding \(C \hookrightarrow \PP^N_k\) for some \(N\)). Pulling the ample line
622	  bundle back along \(C \times_k T \to C\) produces a relatively very ample line
623	  bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\).
624	\end{proof}
625	
626	\begin{corollary}
627	[Flattening stratification for a coherent sheaf on a relative curve]
628	  \label{cor:flattening_stratification_curve}
629	  
630	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a smooth proper curve over \(k\), and \(T\) a noetherian
631	  \(k\)-scheme.  Let \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(C \times_k T\). Then the
632	  flattening-stratification conclusion of
633	  Theorem~REF applies: there is a finite
634	  set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials and locally-closed subschemes
635	  \(T_f \hookrightarrow T\) for \(f \in I\), set-theoretically covering \(T\)
636	  disjointly, such that (a) \(\F|_{C \times_k T_f}\) is flat over \(T_f\) for
637	  each \(f\), and (b) any morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) such that
638	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(C \times_k T'\) is \(T'\)-flat factors uniquely through some
639	  \(T_f\).
640	\end{corollary}
641	
642	\begin{proof}
643	  
644	  By Lemma~REF, \(C \times_k T \to T\) is
645	  projective, hence factors through a closed immersion into \(\PP^n_T\) for some
646	  \(n \ge 0\).  Push \(\F\) forward along the closed immersion (extending by zero
647	  outside the image) to a coherent sheaf \(\tilde \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\); this
648	  operation preserves \(T\)-flatness in both directions because the closed
649	  immersion is a finite morphism.  Apply
650	  Theorem~REF to \(\tilde \F\) on
651	  \(\PP^n_T\) to obtain \(\{T_f\}\) with the stated universal property.
652	\end{proof}
653	
654	\section{Mathlib status}
655	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
656	
657	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides:
658	\begin{itemize}
659	  \item \texttt{Module.Flat} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}) --- the
660	    module-theoretic notion of flatness, applicable affine-locally to the
661	    stalks \(\F_x\).
662	  \item \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Flat} ---
663	    \texttt{IsFlat} as a morphism property and basic stability lemmas; this is
664	    flatness of the structure morphism, \emph{not} flatness of a quasi-coherent
665	    module over the base.
666	  \item \texttt{Module.FaithfullyFlat} and the descent infrastructure that
667	    consumes it.
668	\end{itemize}
669	
670	\noindent What Mathlib does \emph{not} yet have, and that this chapter will
671	introduce in the new file
672	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}:

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a \(\Spec\) with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-\(1\) specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.

89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	
101	\leanok
102	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
103	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
104	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
105	  Let \(X\) be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
106	  let \(U \subseteq X\) be an open subset. For any \(z \in U\),
107	  \[
108	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
109	  \]
110	  where on the right \(U\) carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
111	  z \in U\rangle\( denotes \)z\( viewed as a point of \)U$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	
116	\leanok
117	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers \(n\), of the
118	  set of \(n\) for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
119	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the relevant
120	  specialisation preorder (with \(\prec\) denoting strict specialisation). It
121	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
122	  strict-generisation chains starting at \(z\) in \(X\) and those starting at the
123	  corresponding point of \(U\).
124	
125	  \emph{Chains in \(U\) embed into \(X\).} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow X\) is
126	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(U\)
127	  then \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(X\), and distinct points of \(U\) remain
128	  distinct in \(X\). Hence every strict-generisation chain in \(U\) starting at
129	  \(z\) lifts to one in \(X\) of the same length.
130	
131	  \emph{Chains in \(X\) starting at \(z\) all lie in \(U\).} Conversely, suppose
132	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) is a strict-generisation chain
133	  in \(X\) with \(a_0 = z \in U\). For each \(i \geq 0\), the relation $a_i \succeq
134	  z\( (in the specialisation preorder) is \)z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
135	  \(a_i\) is in the closure of \(\{z\}\) \emph{viewed from above}; in our
136	  convention the strict order \(a \prec b\) records \(b \rightsquigarrow a\)
137	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so \(a_i \succeq z\) in \(X\) means
138	  \(a_i \rightsquigarrow z\) in \(X\). By the Mathlib lemma
139	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
140	  \(a \rightsquigarrow z\) with \(z\) in an open set \(U\) forces \(a \in U\), since
141	  \(U\) is open and contains \(z\), hence contains every point whose closure
142	  contains \(z\) --- equivalently every \(a\) with \(a \rightsquigarrow z\)),
143	  every \(a_i\) lies in \(U\). Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
144	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
145	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle\( in \)U$ of the same length.
146	
147	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
148	  chains starting at \(z\) (resp.\ at \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\)) and
149	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
153	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
154	
155	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
156	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
157	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
158	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
159	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	
163	\leanok
164	  [Coheight on \(\Spec R\) equals height in \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)]
165	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
166	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
167	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(p \in \Spec R\) (viewed as a point of
168	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
169	  \[
170	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
171	  \]
172	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
173	  prime-ideal chains \(\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}\)
174	  ending at the prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) corresponding to \(p\).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	\leanok
180	  The specialisation preorder on \(\Spec R\) is the dual of the natural
181	  inclusion preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). Concretely, for
182	  \(p, q \in \Spec R\) with underlying primes \(\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}\),
183	  the relation \(p \leq q\) in the specialisation preorder (which is
184	  \(q \rightsquigarrow p\) in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
185	  containment \(\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}\). This is the content of
186	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
187	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
188	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on \(\Spec R\)
189	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)).
190	
191	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of \(p\) in \(\Spec R\)
192	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at \(p\),
193	  i.e.\ ascending chains \(p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the
194	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of \(\mathfrak{p}\) in
195	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\) (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
196	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
197	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
198	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
199	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
200	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
201	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
202	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
203	  identification \(\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}\)
204	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
205	\end{proof}
206	
207	\section{The coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
208	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
209	
210	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
211	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
212	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
213	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
214	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
215	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
216	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
217	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
218	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
219	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
220	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}
223	
224	\leanok
225	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
226	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
228	  
229	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be any point. Then
230	  \[
231	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
232	  \]
233	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at \(z\) equals the coheight of \(z\) in
234	  the underlying topological space of \(X\) (with respect to the specialisation
235	  preorder).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  
240	  \leanok
241	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
242	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
243	
244	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of \(z\).} By
245	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
246	  applied to the open set \(X\) itself, there exists an affine open
247	  \(U \subseteq X\) with \(z \in U\). Write \(\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U\)
248	  for \(z\) viewed as a point of the subscheme \(U\).
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to \(z\) inside \(U\).} Since
251	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\) via the affine equivalence
252	  \(U \simeq \Spec R\) (with \(R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\)), the point
253	  \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\) corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
254	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
255	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,\(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\).
256	
257	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of \(\Gamma(U)\) at
258	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The classical affine-stalk identification says
259	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}\). In Mathlib this is the
260	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
261	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\) as a localisation of \(R\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\) in the
262	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
263	
264	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
265	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The Mathlib lemma
266	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
267	  \[
268	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
269	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
270	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
271	  \]
272	  where \(\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})\) is the prime-ideal height in
273	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). By
274	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
275	  \(\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}\) used in
276	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
277	
278	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from \(U\) to \(X\).} By
279	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to \(R\) and the
280	  affine identification \(U \simeq \Spec R\),
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
283	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
284	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
285	  \]
286	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
287	  \(U \subseteq X\) promotes the right-hand side back to
288	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)\). Concatenating the equalities from
289	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{\(\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1\)}{KrullDimLE 1}}
293	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
294	
295	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
296	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
297	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
298	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-\(1\) case used in
299	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
300	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
301	
302	\begin{lemma}
303	
304	\leanok
305	  [Coheight \(= 1\) forces stalk \(\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1\)]
306	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
308	  
309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be a point with
310	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\). Then the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
311	  has Krull dimension at most \(1\), equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
312	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
313	\end{lemma}
314	
315	\begin{proof}
316	  
317	  \leanok
318	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
319	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\).
320	  The Mathlib predicate \(\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R\) unfolds to
321	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1\), so the inequality \(1 \leq 1\)
322	  discharges the goal.
323	\end{proof}

341	two independent goals:
342	\begin{itemize}
343	  \item The right conjunct \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1\)
344	    is discharged by
345	    \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\) (the standing codim-\(1\) hypothesis
346	    on \(z\)) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
347	    pair \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1\) (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
348	    and \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1\) (also from the bridge, since
349	    \(1 \leq 1\)) gives equality.
350	  \item The left conjunct \(\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
351	    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
352	    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
353	    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
354	    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A \(\to\) IsRegularLocalRing \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\)} at
355	    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is \(\sim 200\)--\(300\)~LOC of
356	    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
357	    chapter).
358	\end{itemize}
359	Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
360	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
361	one remains as a named Mathlib gap.
362	
363	\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
364	hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
365	The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
366	argument
367	\[
368	  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
369	\]
370	at every use site. Because \(Y\) is a prime divisor of \(X\) (so
371	\(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1\) by the definition of a prime
372	divisor as a codim-\(1\) irreducible closed subset),
373	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
374	automatically from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\), so the explicit instance
375	argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.
376	
377	\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-\(1\) infrastructure.} Any subsequent
378	project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-\(1\) point has
379	Krull dim \(\leq 1\)'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-\(1\)
380	argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
381	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
382	The instance form
383	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
384	typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.
385

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	
97	\leanok
98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
101	  
102	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
103	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
104	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
105	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
106	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
107	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
108	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
109	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
110	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
111	
112	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
113	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
114	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
115	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
116	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
117	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
118	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
119	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
120	\end{theorem}
121	
122	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
123	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
124	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
125	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
126	then gives the proof.
127	
128	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
129	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
130	
131	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
132	the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\) at the basepoint \(P_0\). It is the
133	content gated on A.3 (existence of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a representable group
134	scheme).
135	
136	\begin{lemma}\leanok
137	[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
138	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
140	  
141	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
142	  % \(f^P\colon C\to J\) (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
143	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
145	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
146	  %  \(f^P\colon C\to J\) has the following universal property: for any map
147	  %  \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending \(P\)
148	  %  to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that
149	  %  \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
150	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, Summary 6.11, doc p.~107
151	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 113).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
153	  % "SUMMARY 6.11.  Between \((C,P)\) and itself, there is a divisorial
154	  %  correspondence \(\mathcal L^P = \mathcal L(\Delta-\{P\}\times C-C\times\{P\})\).
155	  %    Between \((C,P)\) and \(J\) there is the divisorial correspondence
156	  %  \(\mathcal M^P\); for any divisorial correspondence \(\mathcal L\) between
157	  %  \((C,P)\) and a pointed \(k\)-scheme \((T,t)\), there is a unique morphism
158	  %  of pointed \(k\)-schemes \(\varphi\colon T\to J\) such that
159	  %  \((1\times \varphi)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L\).  In particular,
160	  %  there is a unique map \(f^P\colon C\to J\) such that
161	  %  \((1\times f^P)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L^P\) and \(f(P)=0\)."
162	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1 and
163	  Summary~6.11, \S III.6.}
164	
165	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\), let
166	  \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\). There is a
167	  unique regular morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
168	  \[
169	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J, \qquad
170	    Q \;\longmapsto\; \bigl[\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\bigr],
171	  \]
172	  sending the basepoint \(P_0\) to the identity element \(\eta_J \in J(\bar k)\)
173	  and characterised on \(T\)-points by the formula
174	  \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\), where
175	  $\mathcal L^{P_0} := \mathcal O_{C \times C}\bigl(\Delta - \{P_0\} \times C -
176	  C \times \{P_0\}\bigr)$ is the rigidified diagonal correspondence on
177	  \(C \times C\) and \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) is the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence
178	  on \(C \times J\).
179	\end{lemma}
180	
181	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (no separate verbatim source; the existence of
182	% \(f^P\) is part of Milne's standing setup at the head of \S III.6 -- ``\(J\)
183	% will be its Jacobian variety'' -- and is the moduli-of-degree-0-line-bundles
184	% interpretation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) packaged into Summary~6.11.)
185	\begin{proof}
186	  
187	  By A.3, \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the moduli scheme of degree-zero line bundles
188	  on \(C\); concretely, for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) its \(T\)-points are
189	  isomorphism classes of line bundles \(\mathcal L\) on \(C \times_{\bar k} T\)
190	  whose fibre degree at every geometric point of \(T\) is zero, taken modulo line
191	  bundles pulled back from \(T\) (the universal property of the Picard scheme
192	  packaged in \cref{def:pic_scheme}; the identity component imposes the
193	  degree-zero condition).
194	
195	  Apply this to \(T := C\) and the line bundle
196	  \[
197	    \mathcal L^{P_0} \;:=\; \mathcal O_{C \times_{\bar k} C}\bigl(\Delta -
198	      \{P_0\} \times C - C \times \{P_0\}\bigr),
199	  \]
200	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on \(C \times_{\bar k} C\). Its fibre
201	  over a \(\bar k\)-point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) along the second projection is the
202	  degree-zero line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\) on \(C\), so \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\)
203	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a \(C\)-point of \(J\). The
204	  representability of \(J\) at \(T = C\) produces the unique morphism
205	  \[
206	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
207	  \]
208	  such that \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
209	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) on \(C \times J\).
210	
211	  At the basepoint, restriction of \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\) to \(C \times \{P_0\}\)
212	  gives \(\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C\), the trivial line bundle,
213	  whose moduli class in \(J(\bar k)\) is the identity element \(\eta_J\). Hence
214	  \(\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J\). Uniqueness of \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the moduli
215	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism \(\iota'_{P_0}\)
216	  satisfying \((1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
217	  and the pointed condition agrees with \(\iota_{P_0}\) on \(T\)-points for every
218	  \(T\), hence equals \(\iota_{P_0}\) by the Yoneda embedding.
219	\end{proof}
220	
221	\section{The \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)}
222	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g}
223	
224	The Albanese universal property is built from \(C\) by taking the
225	\(g\)-th \emph{symmetric power} \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) -- the quotient of the
226	\(g\)-fold Cartesian power \(C^g\) by the natural action of the symmetric group
227	\(S_g\). We collect the definition here as the first sub-lemma block.
228	
229	\begin{definition}\leanok
230	[Symmetric power of a curve]
231	  \label{def:symmetric_power_curve}
232	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}
233	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~3.1, \S III.3,
234	  % doc p.~94 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 100).
235	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
236	  % "For any variety \(V\), the symmetric group \(S_r\) on \(r\) letters acts on
237	  %  the product of \(r\) copies \(V^r\) of \(V\) by permuting the factors, and
238	  %  we want to define the \(r\)th symmetric power \(V^{(r)}\) of \(V\) to be the
239	  %  quotient \(S_r \backslash V^r\).  The next proposition demonstrates the
240	  %  existence of \(V^{(r)}\) and lists its main properties.
241	  %    A morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) is said to be {\bf symmetric} if
242	  %  \(\varphi\sigma=\varphi\) for all \(\sigma\) in \(S_r\).
243	  %  PROPOSITION 3.1. Let \(V\) be a variety over \(k\).  Then there exists a
244	  %  variety \(V^{(r)}\) and a symmetric morphism \(\pi\colon V^r\to V^{(r)}\)
245	  %  having the following properties:
246	  %    (a) as a topological space, \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) is the quotient of \(V^r\)
247	  %        by \(S_r\);
248	  %    (b) for any open affine subset \(U\) of \(V\), \(U^{(r)}\) is an open
249	  %        affine subset of \(V^{(r)}\) and
250	  %             $\Gamma(U^{(r)},\mathcal O_{V^{(r)}}) = \Gamma(U^r,
251	  %             \mathcal O_{V^r})^{S_r}$
252	  %        (set of elements fixed by the action of \(S_r\)).
253	  %  The pair \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) has the following universal property: every
254	  %  symmetric \(k\)-morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) factors uniquely
255	  %  through \(\pi\).
256	  %  The map \(\pi\) is finite, surjective, and separable."
257	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.1, \S III.3, p.~94.}
258	
259	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over an algebraically closed field
260	  \(\bar k\) and let \(g \ge 1\). The \emph{\(g\)-th symmetric power}
261	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is a \(\bar k\)-scheme equipped with a finite surjective
262	  symmetric morphism \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) characterised by
263	  the following universal property: for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) and every
264	  symmetric \(\bar k\)-morphism \(\varphi \colon C^g \to T\) (i.e.\ a morphism
265	  satisfying \(\varphi \circ \sigma = \varphi\) for all \(\sigma \in S_g\)
266	  acting by permutation of the factors of \(C^g\)) there exists a unique
267	  morphism \(\overline\varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to T\) with
268	  \(\overline\varphi \circ \pi = \varphi\). As a topological space
269	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is the quotient of \(C^g\) by \(S_g\), and for every open
270	  affine \(U \subseteq C\) the open subset $U^{(g)} \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g
271	  C\( is affine with global sections the \)S_g$-invariants
272	  \(\Gamma(U^g, \mathcal O_{C^g})^{S_g}\).
273	\end{definition}
274	
275	\medskip
276	
277	\textbf{Mathlib status.} Mathlib has no formalised \(g\)-th symmetric power of
278	a scheme: only \texttt{Sym}/\texttt{SymmetricPower} for types and modules.
279	The corresponding scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied
280	project-side. The intended Lean construction follows Milne's recipe of
281	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}: on affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq C\)
282	form \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\), then glue via the standard affine
283	patching for an open cover \(C = \bigcup U_i\). This sub-build is a
284	non-trivial dependency of A.4.d that the prover lane must absorb before the
285	main universal property closes; a \texttt{\textbackslash notready} marker is
286	appropriate on the corresponding Lean target until the construction lands.
287	For the proof of Proposition~3.1 itself, Milne refers to Mumford 1970,
288	\S II~\S 7, p.~66 and \S III \S 11, p.~112, for the patching details (the
289	quoted reference at the end of Milne's Proof on \S III.3, p.~94).
290	
291	\section{The morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)}
292	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_av_map}
293	
294	The second sub-lemma is the elementary fact that the symmetric assignment
295	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) from
296	\(C^g\) to \(A\) descends through \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\). The
297	key point is that the addition morphism \(A^g \to A\) is itself
298	\(S_g\)-symmetric because \(A\) is commutative as a group scheme.
299	
300	\begin{lemma}\leanok
301	[Symmetrisation of a curve-to-AV morphism]
302	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}
304	  
305	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
306	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "PROOF.  Consider the map
309	  %     \((P_1,...,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\)
310	  %  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\)."
311	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
312	  \S III.6, p.~104.}
313	
314	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
315	  over \(\bar k\). The composition
316	  \[
317	    \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}
318	    \;\colon\; C^g \;\longrightarrow\; A^g \;\longrightarrow\; A,
319	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\; \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g),
320	  \]
321	  is \(S_g\)-equivariant (where \(S_g\) acts on \(C^g\) by permutation of the
322	  factors and on \(A\) trivially). Consequently, by the universal property of
323	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) (\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}), there is a unique
324	  morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
325	  \[
326	    \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A
327	  \]
328	  such that
329	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ \pi = \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\).
330	\end{lemma}
331	
332	\begin{proof}
333	  
334	  Since \(A\) is a commutative group scheme, the \(g\)-fold addition morphism
335	  \(\mathrm{add}_A \colon A^g \to A\) satisfies $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau =
336	  \mathrm{add}_A\( for every \)\tau \in S_g\( permuting the factors of \)A^g$
337	  (this is the scheme-theoretic statement that summation in a commutative
338	  monoid is independent of the order of summands; on \(T\)-points it is the
339	  identity \(a_1 + \cdots + a_g = a_{\tau(1)} + \cdots + a_{\tau(g)}\) in the
340	  abelian group \(A(T)\)). The diagonal action of \(\tau\) on $\varphi^{\times
341	  g} \colon C^g \to A^g$ is also given by permutation of factors. Therefore
342	  \[
343	    (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}) \circ \tau
344	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\varphi^{\times g} \circ \tau)
345	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\tau \circ \varphi^{\times g})
346	    \;=\; (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau) \circ \varphi^{\times g}
347	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g},
348	  \]
349	  so the composition \(\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\) is symmetric
350	  in the sense of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}. The universal property
351	  of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the unique factorisation
352	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\).
353	\end{proof}
354	
355	\section{The birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\)}
356	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_to_jacobian}
357	
358	The third sub-lemma is the birational identification of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)
359	with \(J\). After choosing the basepoint \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\), the cycle map
360	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\)
361	defines a morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) which is
362	birational by Milne III~Theorem~5.1(a).
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Birational morphism from \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to \(J\)]
366	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}
368	  
369	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~5.1(a), \S III.5,
370	  % doc p.~101 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 107).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "Let \(f^r\) be the map \(C^r\to J\) sending \((P_1,...,P_r)\) to
373	  %  \(f(P_1)+\cdots+f(P_r)\).  On points, \(f^r\) is the map
374	  %  \((P_1,...,P_r)\mapsto[P_1+\cdots+P_r-rP]\).  Clearly it is symmetric,
375	  %  and so induces a map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to J\).  We can regard
376	  %  \(f^{(r)}\) as being the map sending an effective divisor \(D\) of degree
377	  %  \(r\) on \(C\) to the linear equivalence class of \(D-rP\).  The fibre of
378	  %  the map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}(k)\to J(k)\) containing \(D\) can be
379	  %  identified with the space of effective divisors linearly equivalent
380	  %  to \(D\), that is, with the linear system \(|D|\).  The image of \(C^{(r)}\)
381	  %  in \(J\) is a closed subvariety \(W^r\) of \(J\), which can also be written
382	  %  \(W^r=f(C)+\cdots+f(C)\) (\(r\) summands).
383	  %  THEOREM 5.1.  (a) For all \(r\le g\), the morphism
384	  %  \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to W^r\) is birational; in particular,
385	  %  \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational map from \(C^{(g)}\) onto \(J\)."
386	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~5.1(a) and the
387	  preceding paragraph, \S III.5, p.~101.}
388	
389	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\),
390	  let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\),
391	  \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
392	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The regular morphism
393	  \(\iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J^g\) composed with the \(g\)-fold
394	  group-law addition \(\mathrm{add}_J \colon J^g \to J\) descends through
395	  \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to a regular morphism
396	  \[
397	    f^{(g)} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; J,
398	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\;
399	      \bigl[\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)\bigr].
400	  \]
401	  The morphism \(f^{(g)}\) is birational: it is dominant (its image is
402	  \(J\), the \(g\)-fold sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) exhausts
403	  \(J\) since \(\dim J = g\) and the iterated sum reaches full dimension), and
404	  its generic fibre is a single \(\bar k\)-point (a degree-\(g\) effective
405	  divisor \(D\) on \(C\) with \(h^0(D) = 1\) is the unique element of its linear
406	  system \(|D|\); such divisors form a dense open subset of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
407	  C\( -- a consequence of \)\dim |D| = 0\( generically on a curve of genus \)g$
408	  by Riemann--Roch). Equivalently, \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational morphism in
409	  the sense that there exist dense opens
410	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\), \(V \subseteq J\) such that
411	  \(f^{(g)}|_U \colon U \to V\) is an isomorphism.
412	\end{lemma}
413	
414	\begin{proof}
415	  
416	  Symmetry of \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g}\) is the
417	  specialisation of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} to the abelian
418	  variety \(A := J\) and the morphism \(\varphi := \iota_{P_0}\); the universal
419	  property of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the regular morphism \(f^{(g)}\).
420	  The closed-form description on \(\bar k\)-points,
421	  \(f^{(g)}(P_1, \ldots, P_g) = [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\),
422	  follows because \(\iota_{P_0}(P_i) = [\mathcal O_C(P_i - P_0)]\) by
423	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} and the group law on \(J\) is the
424	  tensor-product of degree-zero line bundles.
425	
426	  We verify birationality. The image of \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is the iterated
427	  sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) summands), which is
428	  closed (it is the image of the proper morphism
429	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) being proper because \(C^g\)
430	  is proper and \(\pi\) is finite surjective). By the dimension count
431	  \(\dim \mathrm{Sym}^g C = g = \dim J\) (Riemann--Roch and the standing
432	  hypothesis \(g > 0\)), the image \(W^g \subseteq J\) has dimension at most
433	  \(g\), and equality forces \(W^g = J\) since \(J\) is irreducible. Hence
434	  \(f^{(g)}\) is surjective with \(\dim\)-equal source and target.
435	
436	  For the generic-fibre identification, Riemann--Roch
437	  \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) at \(\deg D = g\) gives $h^0(D) - h^1(D)
438	  = 1\(, so for a general \)D \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\( with \)h^1(D) =
439	  0\( (the locus where \)h^1$ jumps is closed of positive codimension on a
440	  smooth curve, by upper semicontinuity of cohomology), we have $h^0(D) =
441	  1\( and the linear system \)|D|\( consists of \)D$ alone. Therefore the
442	  fibre of \(f^{(g)}\) over the generic point of \(J\) contains a single point
443	  \(D\), i.e.\ \(f^{(g)}\) is birational onto \(J\).
444	
445	  By \cref{def:pic_scheme} the underlying \(\bar k\)-scheme \(J\) is the
446	  Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) obtained at A.3, so the moduli-theoretic
447	  reading of \(f^{(g)}\) (effective divisor class \(\mapsto\) degree-zero
448	  line bundle class via \(D \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(D - g P_0)]\)) is the
449	  same as the Yoneda image of the \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)-point
450	  $[\mathcal O_{C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g C}(\mathcal D_g - g\{P_0\} \times
451	  \mathrm{Sym}^g C)]\(, where \)\mathcal D_g \subseteq C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g
452	  C\( is the universal effective Cartier divisor of degree \)g\( on \)C$. We
453	  do not need this Yoneda repackaging for the proof of the Albanese UP --
454	  the symmetric-power route uses only the morphism \(f^{(g)}\) and its
455	  birationality.
456	\end{proof}
457	
458	\section{Descent through the birational quotient \(\sigma\)}
459	\label{sec:albaneseup_descent}
460	
461	The final auxiliary lemma packages the two-step descent that promotes a
462	morphism on \(C^g\) (after passing through \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) via
463	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and inverting \(f^{(g)}\) via
464	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}) to a regular morphism on
465	\(J\). The output is a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), which is then
466	upgraded to a regular morphism via Milne's Theorem~3.2
467	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}). This is the unique step of the
468	proof that consumes A.4.c.
469	
470	\begin{lemma}\leanok
471	[Descent of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\)]
472	  \label{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}
473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}
474	  
475	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
476	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
477	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
478	  % "It therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
479	  %  shows to be a regular map."
480	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
481	  \S III.6, p.~104 (the sentence ``which (I~3.2) shows to be a regular
482	  map'').}
483	
484	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
485	  over \(\bar k\). Let \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)
486	  be the symmetrised morphism of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and
487	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) the birational morphism of
488	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}. Then there is a unique
489	  regular morphism
490	  \[
491	    \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A
492	  \]
493	  such that \(\psi \circ f^{(g)} = \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) on the dense open
494	  on which \(f^{(g)}\) is an isomorphism (equivalently, \(\psi\) is the unique
495	  regular morphism extending the rational map
496	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1} \colon J \dashrightarrow
497	  A$).
498	\end{lemma}
499	
500	\begin{proof}
501	  
502	  By \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, there exist dense opens
503	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) and \(V \subseteq J\) with $f^{(g)}|_U \colon
504	  U \xrightarrow{\sim} V\( an isomorphism. Setting \)\psi_0 \colon V \to A$
505	  to be the composition $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi|_U \circ (f^{(g)}|_U)^{-1}
506	  \colon V \to U \to A\( produces a rational map \)J \dashrightarrow A$.
507	
508	  The target \(A\) is an abelian variety and the source \(J\) is smooth over
509	  \(\bar k\) (it is itself an abelian variety, hence nonsingular). Milne's
510	  Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) therefore promotes
511	  \(\psi_0\) to a unique regular morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) extending
512	  \(\psi_0\) on \(V\). Uniqueness of \(\psi\) follows from the
513	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle: two regular morphisms
514	  \(J \to A\) agreeing on a dense open are equal.
515	\end{proof}
516	
517	\section{Proof of the universal property}
518	\label{sec:albaneseup_proof}
519	
520	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
521	% "PROOF.  Consider the map
522	%    \(\)(P_1,\ldots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\(\)
523	%  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\).  It
524	%  therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
525	%  shows to be a regular map.  It is clear from the construction that
526	%  \(\psi\circ f^P=\varphi\) (note that \(f^P\) is the composite of $Q\mapsto
527	%  Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}\( with \)f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J$).  In
528	%  particular, \(\psi\) maps 0 to 0, and (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore
529	%  a homomorphism.  If \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism such that
530	%  \(\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi\), then \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on
531	%  \(f^P(C)+\cdots+f^P(C)\) (\(g\) copies), which is the whole of \(J\)."
532	\begin{proof}[Proof of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}]
533	  
534	  The proof follows Milne's Proposition~6.1 verbatim, using the four
535	  sub-lemmas of this chapter:
536	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} (Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\)),
537	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} (symmetrisation of \(\varphi\) to
538	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)),
539	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian} (birational morphism
540	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\)), and
541	  \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} (descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
542	  \varphi\( to a regular morphism \)\psi \colon J \to A$ via
543	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}).
544	  \begin{enumerate}
545	    \item \emph{Symmetrisation.} Consider the map \(C^g \to A\) sending
546	      \((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\)
547	      (Milne's typeset proof writes \(\sum_i \psi(P_i)\) in this line, but
548	      the construction is the symmetrisation of the input \(\varphi\); we
549	      use the unambiguous symbol \(\varphi\) throughout). Since \(A\) is
550	      commutative this map is \(S_g\)-symmetric. By
551	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} it factors through the
552	      symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\), defining a regular morphism
553	      \[
554	        \widetilde \psi \;:=\; \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi
555	        \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A.
556	      \]
557	    \item \emph{Descent to a regular morphism on \(J\).} By
558	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, the morphism
559	      \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) is birational. The
560	      composite \(\widetilde \psi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1}\) is, a priori,
561	      only a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\). By
562	      \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} -- whose proof is an
563	      application of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne
564	      Theorem~I~3.2, rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
565	      abelian variety is regular) -- this rational map extends uniquely
566	      to a regular morphism
567	      \[
568	        \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A.
569	      \]
570	    \item \emph{Factorisation \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\).}
571	      Milne's identification: \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the composite
572	      \[
573	        C \;\xrightarrow{\;Q \mapsto Q + (g-1) P_0\;}\;
574	        \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\xrightarrow{\;f^{(g)}\;}\; J,
575	      \]
576	      where the first arrow sends a point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) to the
577	      effective divisor \(Q + (g-1) P_0 \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\)
578	      (its image under \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is
579	      $[\mathcal O_C(Q + (g-1) P_0 - g P_0)] = [\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)]
580	      = \iota_{P_0}(Q)$, matching \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}). On
581	      \(\bar k\)-points,
582	      \[
583	        \psi(\iota_{P_0}(Q)) = \widetilde\psi(Q + (g-1) P_0)
584	          = \varphi(Q) + (g-1)\varphi(P_0) = \varphi(Q),
585	      \]
586	      using \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\) and that \(\eta_A\) is the identity of
587	      \(A\). Hence \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) on \(\bar k\)-points,
588	      and therefore \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) as morphisms of
589	      \(\bar k\)-schemes (both sides are regular morphisms \(C \to A\) from
590	      a reduced separated scheme to a separated scheme, agreeing on
591	      dense \(\bar k\)-points, hence agreeing everywhere by the
592	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle).
593	    \item \emph{Homomorphism property.} The morphism \(\psi\) sends
594	      \(\eta_J\) to \(\eta_A\): indeed,
595	      \(\psi(\eta_J) = \psi(\iota_{P_0}(P_0)) = \varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\).
596	      A regular morphism of abelian varieties sending the identity to
597	      the identity is a homomorphism of group schemes, by Milne
598	      Corollary~I~1.2 (a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma
599	      \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, see
600	      \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, Corollary~1.2). Therefore
601	      \(\psi\) is a homomorphism of group schemes \(J \to A\).
602	    \item \emph{Uniqueness.} Suppose \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism
603	      with \(\psi' \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\). Then \(\psi\) and
604	      \(\psi'\) agree on the set-theoretic image $\iota_{P_0}(C) \subseteq
605	      J$. Both are homomorphisms, so they agree on the subgroup
606	      generated by \(\iota_{P_0}(C)\) -- which contains the \(g\)-fold sum
607	      \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) copies). The
608	      \(g\)-fold sum is the image of
609	      \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J\),
610	      which descends to \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), which
611	      is surjective by \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}.
612	      Therefore \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on a dense open subset of \(J\)
613	      (in fact on all of \(J\) as a set), hence everywhere by the
614	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle.
615	  \end{enumerate}
616	  This completes the proof.
617	\end{proof}
618	
619	\section{Wiring for the positive-genus Albanese witness}
620	\label{sec:albaneseup_witness_wiring}
621	
622	\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} supplies the universal-factorisation
623	field \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\) of an Albanese witness for \((C, P_0)\) in the
624	sense of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}); the remaining
625	fields are supplied by A.3:
626	\begin{itemize}
627	  \item \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) (the underlying scheme).
628	  \item \(\texttt{grpObj}\): the group-object structure on \(J\) obtained from the
629	    abelian-group structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(T)\) for varying \(T\), refined to a
630	    group object in the category of \(\bar k\)-schemes (A.3, A.2.c group-scheme
631	    refinement).
632	  \item \(\texttt{proper}\): properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec \bar k\) from
633	    the closed-immersion property of the identity component in the proper
634	    representing Picard scheme (A.3; cf.\
635	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_proper}'s discussion).
636	  \item \(\texttt{smoothGenus}\): smoothness of relative dimension \(g(C)\) from
637	    the deformation-theoretic tangent-space identification with $H^1(C,
638	    \mathcal O_C)\(, which has dimension \)g(C)$ by Riemann--Roch.
639	  \item \(\texttt{geomIrred}\): geometric irreducibility of the identity
640	    component of a \(\bar k\)-group scheme locally of finite type (Kleiman
641	    Lem.~lem:agps and Prp.~prp:pic0; cf.\
642	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}'s discussion).
643	  \item \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\): the present
644	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}, applied at each
645	    \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\).
646	\end{itemize}
647	
648	The witness so produced is over \(\bar k\). The Galois-descent step from $\bar
649	k\( back to the original base field \)k$ -- packaged at
650	\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f -- closes the loop and supplies the
651	\(k\)-side Albanese witness consumed by \cref{def:Jacobian}.
652	
653	\section{Lean encoding}
654	\label{sec:albaneseup_lean_encoding}
655	
656	The Lean target file is the new
657	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean} (declared at the top of
658	this chapter). The five target declarations are:
659	\begin{itemize}
660	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}: the regular morphism
661	    \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}.
662	    The signature exposes the marked point \(P_0\) as an explicit argument and
663	    the Picard-scheme construction \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an existing object.
664	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}: the scheme-theoretic
665	    \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
666	    packaged with the canonical projection
667	    \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) and its universal property
668	    (factorisation of every \(S_g\)-symmetric morphism out of \(C^g\)). \emph{This
669	    declaration has no Mathlib analogue}: Mathlib's \texttt{Sym} /
670	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} cover types and modules only; the
671	    scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied project-side, following
672	    Milne's affine-and-glue recipe of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}.
673	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}: the
674	    symmetrised morphism
675	    \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\) produced by
676	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} from an input morphism
677	    \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) into an abelian variety.
678	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}: the
679	    birational morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) of
680	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, together with the data of a
681	    dense open isomorphism \(U \xrightarrow{\sim} V\) realising
682	    birationality. The signature exposes the basepoint \(P_0\) and consumes
683	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}.
684	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}: the
685	    descent lemma of \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma},
686	    consuming \texttt{symmetricPowerAVMap} and
687	    \texttt{symmetricPowerToJacobian} together with
688	    \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
689	    (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) to produce the regular
690	    morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\).
691	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese\_universal\_property}: the
692	    main theorem, the existence-and-uniqueness statement of
693	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}. The signature is
694	    \[
695	      \texttt{P\_0 : C(\bar k)} \;\to\;
696	      \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
697	      (\varphi : C \to A) \;\to\;
698	      (h\varphi : \varphi \circ P_0 = \eta_A) \;\to\;
699	      \exists ! \, \psi \colon J \to A, \;\; \psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi
700	      \;\wedge\; \psi \text{ is a group homomorphism}.
701	    \]
702	\end{itemize}
703	
704	The proof body of \texttt{albanese\_universal\_property} consumes its four
705	sub-lemma exports plus \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
706	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) and Milne's Corollary~I~1.2
707	(\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} consequence, registered at
708	\cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}); the wiring follows the five-step proof
709	of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}.
710	
711	The Picard-scheme inputs (\cref{def:pic_scheme} of
712	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) are imported from
713	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} (when that file
714	materialises along the A.2.c row) and the identity-component / degree-map

716	chapter; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'' below). At the time of writing, the
717	prover-side declaration body
718	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albane\-se\_universal\_property} should be

725	\section{Out of scope}
726	\label{sec:albaneseup_out_of_scope}
727	
728	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Albanese universal property
729	(\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} = Milne Proposition~III~6.1) and the
730	four internal sub-lemmas of the symmetric-power proof
731	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism},
732	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
733	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map},
734	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
735	\cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}), plus the wiring of these into
736	a positive-genus Albanese witness. The following adjacent content is
737	explicitly out of scope:
738	\begin{itemize}
739	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (A.2.c).} Proved in
740	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} as
741	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}; this chapter only consumes it.
742	  \item \textbf{The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and the degree map
743	    (A.3).} The identity component refinement of the Picard scheme together
744	    with its degree map is the subject of a forthcoming chapter; this chapter
745	    consumes \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
746	    \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\) but does not construct it.
747	  \item \textbf{Milne's Theorem~3.2 (A.4.c).} Proved in
748	    \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
749	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only consumes it
750	    inside \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} to promote the
751	    rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism.
752	  \item \textbf{The scheme-theoretic construction of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\).}
753	    Mathlib has no \(g\)-th symmetric power of a scheme; the
754	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} target of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve} is a
755	    project-side sub-build (affine \(S_g\)-invariants
756	    \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\) on each open affine \(\Spec A \subseteq C\),
757	    glued by the standard open-cover patching of Milne~III.3
758	    Proposition~3.1 / Mumford~1970 II.7 \& III.11). The construction itself
759	    is a non-trivial dependency of A.4.d; this chapter \emph{specifies} the
760	    target but does not develop its construction.
761	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(J \cong J^\vee\) (Milne~III \S 6.6) and the
762	    moduli / Poincar\'e-bundle Albanese route.} The iter-174 draft of this
763	    chapter proposed a route via the Poincar\'e bundle of \(A\) and the
764	    autoduality of \(J\), which gates on the theorem of the cube. The cube
765	    was excised from the project in iter-163; the autoduality / moduli
766	    route is therefore demoted and replaced by the symmetric-power route
767	    of this chapter. A reference recording of the moduli wording is given
768	    in the ``Alternative route history'' \texttt{\%~NOTE} block at the end
769	    of this file.
770	  \item \textbf{Milne Proposition~III~6.4 / Remark~6.5 / Theorem~6.6} (the
771	    divisorial-correspondence universal property for \(C \times C \to A\),
772	    the identification of \((J, F)\) with the Albanese variety in the sense
773	    of Lang, and the autoduality \(J \cong J^\vee\)). Not needed by the
774	    symmetric-power route.
775	  \item \textbf{Weil's reconstruction of \(J\) from the rational law of
776	    composition on \(C^{(g)}\) (Milne~III \S 7).} The project does not need
777	    this alternative construction.
778	  \item \textbf{The genus-\(0\) case.} The standing hypothesis of this chapter
779	    is \(g(C) > 0\) (Milne's standing hypothesis of \S III.6). The genus-\(0\)
780	    case of the Albanese witness is supplied by
781	    \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}), routed through
782	    \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} and the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
783	    shortcut; that construction is independent of and disjoint from the
784	    Pic\(^0\)-route of this chapter.
785	  \item \textbf{Generalisations to a non-algebraically-closed base \(k\).}
786	    The chapter works over \(\bar k\) throughout. The descent of the witness
787	    back to \(k\) via Galois descent of morphism equality is the subject of
788	    \cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f.
789	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
