12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.

69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\)

83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}

183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}

278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}

362	
363	\begin{lemma}\leanok
364	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
365	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
367	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
368	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
369	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
370	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
371	  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
372	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
373	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
374	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
375	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
376	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
377	  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
378	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
379	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
380	  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
381	  \[
382	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
383	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
384	  \]
385	  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
386	  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
387	  \[
388	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
389	  \]
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
394	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
395	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
396	  \leanok
397	  By the definition of \(\chi\)
398	  (REF),
399	  \[
400	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
401	    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
402	  \]
403	  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements
404	  (\cref{lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one} and
405	  \cref{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}), attacked separately
406	  by the prover.
407	
408	  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
409	  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
410	  \[
411	    H^0(C, k(P))
412	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
413	    \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\!\bigl(
414	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\,
415	      k(P)
416	    \bigr)
417	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
418	    \Gamma(C, k(P))
419	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
420	    (k(P))(C),
421	  \]
422	  realised by the project lemmas
423	  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
424	  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
425	  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top} (the last step
426	  identifies the abstract \(\Gamma\) functor with the underlying
427	  presheaf evaluation at the top open \(\top\), i.e.\ at the whole
428	  underlying space of \(C\)). Applying
429	  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
430	  goal to
431	  \(\dim_{\bar k} (k(P))(C) = 1\). The closed point
432	  \(P\) lies in the top open of \(C\), so the skyscraper presheaf
433	  evaluates at the top open to its stalk
434	  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
435	  \(\dim_{\bar k} \bar k = 1\) closes the half. This half is
436	  axiom-clean closure projection (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against
437	  the existing project chain; it is the natural target for the
438	  iteration that follows the present blueprint expansion.
439	
440	  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
441	  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
442	  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
443	  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
444	  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
445	  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
446	  \(\bar k\)-vector space.
447	
448	  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
449	  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
450	  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
451	  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
452	  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
453	  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
454	  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
455	  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
456	  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
457	  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
458	  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
459	  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
460	  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
461	  % gap to clear.
462	
463	  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
464	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
465	\end{proof}

467	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
468	
469	\begin{theorem}
470	
471	
472	\leanok
473	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
474	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
476	  
477	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
478	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
479	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
480	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
481	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
482	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
483	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
485	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
486	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
487	  % is the Euler characteristic
488	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
489	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
490	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
491	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
492	  \[
493	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
494	  \]
495	\end{theorem}
496	
497	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
498	% formula says
499	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
500	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
501	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
502	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
503	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
504	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
505	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
506	% the result holds for all \(D\).
507	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
508	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
509	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
510	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
511	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
512	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
513	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
514	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
515	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
516	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
517	% = 1$, so we have
518	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
519	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
520	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \leanok
523	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
524	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
525	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
526	
527	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
528	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
529	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
530	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
531	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
532	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
533	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
534	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
535	
536	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
537	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
538	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
539	  \[
540	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
541	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
542	    \;=\; 1 - g.
543	  \]
544	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
545	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
546	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
547	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
548	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
549	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
550	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
551	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
552	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
553	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
554	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
555	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
556	
557	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
558	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
559	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
560	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
561	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
562	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
563	  the short exact sequence
564	  \[
565	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
566	  \]
567	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
568	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
569	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
570	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
571	  \[
572	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
573	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
574	    \to k(P) \to 0.
575	  \]
576	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
577	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
578	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
579	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
580	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
581	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
582	  REF.)
583	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
584	  (REF, the project-side
585	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
586	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
589	    \;=\;
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
591	  \]
592	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
593	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
594	  REF (factored from the
595	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
596	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
597	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
598	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
599	  body). Therefore
600	  \[
601	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
602	  \]
603	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
604	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
605	  REF
606	  \[
607	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
608	  \]
609	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
610	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
611	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
612	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
613	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
614	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
615	
616	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
617	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
618	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
619	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
620	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
621	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
622	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
623	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
624	  REF (\(\chi\) on
625	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
626	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
627	  identity REF
628	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
629	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
630	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
631	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
632	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
633	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},
634	  \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar}) but does \emph{not} ship the
635	  specific additivity statement
636	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} at the
637	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
638	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
639	\end{proof}

641	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
642	
643	\begin{theorem}
644	
645	
646	\leanok
647	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
648	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
650	  
651	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
652	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
653	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
655	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
656	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
657	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
658	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
659	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
660	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
661	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
662	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
663	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
664	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
665	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
666	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
667	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
668	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
669	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
670	  \[
671	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
672	  \]
673	\end{theorem}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \leanok
677	  
678	  Specialising
679	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
680	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
681	  \[
682	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
683	  \]
684	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
685	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
686	  (REF), this rewrites as
687	  \[
688	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
689	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
690	  \]
691	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
692	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
693	  \[
694	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
695	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
696	  \]
697	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
698	
699	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
700	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
701	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
702	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
703	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
704	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
705	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
706	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
707	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
708	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
709	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
710	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
711	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
712	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
713	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
714	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
715	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
716	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
717	  follow-up signature simplification.
718	
719	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
720	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
721	  REF of
722	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
723	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
724	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
725	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
726	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
727	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
728	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
729	\end{proof}
730	
731	\section{Out of scope}
732	
733	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
734	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
735	
736	\begin{itemize}
737	  \item The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch theorem
738	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
739	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor \(K\), the
740	    canonical sheaf \(\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C\), and
741	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
742	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
743	    the genus-\(0\) critical path does not require them; the general
744	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
745	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
746	    from a Weil divisor \(D\), the linear-equivalence isomorphism
747	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), and
748	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
749	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
750	    \(t\) exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
751	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
752	    of REF the dependency on
753	    \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is reduced to its \(H^0\) functor and the short
754	    exact sequence of the closed point.
755	  \item The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for
756	    \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve is consumed as a named
757	    premise by REF; its closure is
758	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
759	    \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)\) is computed) and is recovered for
760	    a general smooth proper genus-\(0\) curve via the isomorphism
761	    \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
762	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
763	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
764	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to
765	    \(\mathbb P^1\)'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
766	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
767	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
768	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
769	    \deg(D) + 1\( identity); the other input is the degree-\)0$
770	    principal-divisor identity REF from
771	    \texttt{RR.1}.
772	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping (\(\chi\) as an alternating sum
773	    over all \(i \geq 0\), the Grothendieck vanishing
774	    \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i > \dim C = 1\)) is not separately
775	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
776	    \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\) which is the standing
777	    curve specialisation.
778	\end{itemize}

12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
14	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
15	\[
16	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
17	  \,\colon\,
18	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
19	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
20	\]
21	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
22	\begin{itemize}
23	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
24	    group \(\Div(C)\), the degree map
25	    \(\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z\) (\texttt{def:divisor_degree}), and the

27	    (\texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero}).

29	    formula in genus zero \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\)
30	    (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
31	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
32	    the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\), its global-sections identification
33	    \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), and the corollary
34	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
35	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
36	    \bar k \cdot 1\( on a genus-\)0\( curve with a marked \)\bar k$-point.
37	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
38	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\)
39	    and a \(\bar k\)-rational point is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).''
40	\end{itemize}
41	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
42	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
43	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as

49	\paragraph{Scope of the \(\bar k\)-base classification.} The chapter restricts
50	to the case where the base field \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
51	k\( every closed point is \)\bar k\(-rational, so the ``\)C\( has a \)\bar
52	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
53	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) is non-empty,
54	hence has closed points, all of which are \(\bar k\)-rational). The general
55	``\(k \to \bar k\) descent'' step ``genus-\(0\) curve over a non-closed \(k\) with

58	consumer) and is out of scope here.

80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	\leanok
84	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
85	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
86	  
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
89	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
90	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
91	  \[
92	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
93	  \]
94	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
95	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
96	  \[
97	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
98	  \]
99	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
100	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
101	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
102	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
103	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
104	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
106	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
107	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
108	\end{lemma}
109	\begin{proof}
110	
111	
112	\leanok
113	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
114	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
115	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
116	  under the name
117	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
118	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
119	  \begin{quote}
120	  \small
121	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
122	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
123	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
124	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
125	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
126	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
127	  \end{quote}
128	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
129	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
130	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
131	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
132	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
133	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
134	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
135	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
136	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
137	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
138	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
139	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
140	  project-bespoke wrapper
141	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
142	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
143	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
144	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
145	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
146	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
147	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
148	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
149	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
150	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
151	\end{proof}
152	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
153	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
154	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
155	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
156	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
157	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
158	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
159	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
160	\begin{lemma}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
164	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
165	  
166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
167	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
168	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
169	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
170	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
171	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
172	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
173	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
174	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
175	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
176	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
177	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
178	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
179	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
180	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
181	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
182	  \[
183	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
184	    \quad\text{and}\quad
185	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
186	  \]
187	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
188	  \[
189	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
190	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
191	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
192	  \]
193	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
194	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
195	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
196	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
197	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
198	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
199	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
200	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
201	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
202	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
203	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
204	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
205	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
206	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
207	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
208	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
209	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
210	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
211	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
212	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
213	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
214	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
215	\end{lemma}

217	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
218	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor
219	% of `\varphi^{\#} t_\infty`, i.e. `(div(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0
220	% = \varphi^*[\infty]`. The supporting Weil-divisor `positivePart`
221	% definition lives in `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` as

227	% is `Hom.poleDivisor \varphi = positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#}
228	% t_\infty))`, and the degree identity is a one-line corollary of
229	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. iter-190 prover lane
230	% (Lane I) implements the refactor:
231	%   1. New def `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` in `WeilDivisor.lean`
232	%      (~10-15 LOC; lattice `D \sqcup 0`).
233	%   2. New lemma
234	%      `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` as a

237	%      owed iter-191+).
238	%   3. Refactor `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` body in `RationalCurveIso.lean`
239	%      from `principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg` to
240	%      `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg)` (~5 LOC).
241	%   4. Close `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body via a 1-line
242	%      rewrite consuming (2) (~3 LOC).
243	% Net iter-190 Lane I: 1 axiom-clean closure (Pin 2 body, modulo named
244	% typed sorry on (2)); 1 NEW named typed-sorry pin
245	% (`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`) which is itself an
246	% iter-191+ Hartshorne II.6.9 substrate target. See iter-190 sidecar
247	% `iter/iter-190/plan.md` for the full rationale.
248	\begin{proof}
249	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
250	  \leanok
251	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
252	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
253	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
254	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
255	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
256	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
257	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
258	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
259	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
260	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
261	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
262	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
263	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
264	  is finite.
265	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
266	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
267	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
268	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
269	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
270	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
271	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
272	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
273	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
274	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
275	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
276	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
277	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
278	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
279	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
280	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
281	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
282	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
283	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
284	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
285	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
286	\end{proof}
287	\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
288	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
289	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
290	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
291	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to \(\varphi\)) was strictly

300	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer; refreshed iter-196): The Lean
301	% `iso_of_degree_one` body inline Step 1 was refactored iter-194 from
302	% 16 LOC of `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one +

318	\begin{lemma}
319	
320	\leanok
321	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
322	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
323	  
324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
325	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
326	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
327	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
328	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
329	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
330	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
331	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
332	  % equivalent:}
333	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
334	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
335	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
336	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
337	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
338	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
339	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
340	  (p.~299).}
341	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
342	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
343	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
344	  degree one,
345	  \[
346	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
347	  \]
348	  formally
349	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
350	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
351	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
352	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
353	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
354	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
355	  \[
356	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
357	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
358	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
359	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
360	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
361	  \]
362	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
363	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
364	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
365	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
366	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
367	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
368	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
369	\end{lemma}
370	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
371	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
372	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
373	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
374	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
375	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
376	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
377	\begin{proof}
378	
379	
380	\leanok
381	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
382	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
383	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
384	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
385	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
386	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
387	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
388	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
389	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
390	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
391	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
392	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
393	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
394	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
395	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
396	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
397	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
398	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
399	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
400	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
401	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
402	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
403	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
404	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
405	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
406	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
407	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
408	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
409	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
410	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
411	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
412	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
413	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
414	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
415	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
416	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
417	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
418	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
419	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
420	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
421	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
422	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
423	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
424	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
425	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
426	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
427	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
428	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
429	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
430	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
431	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
432	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
433	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
434	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
435	\end{proof}
436	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
437	\begin{theorem}
438	
439	\leanok
440	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
441	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
442	  
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
444	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
445	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
446	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
447	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
448	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
449	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
450	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
451	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
452	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
453	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
454	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
455	  \[
456	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
457	  \]
458	  Concretely, the Lean signature
459	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
460	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
461	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
462	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
463	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
464	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
465	\end{theorem}
466	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
467	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
468	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
469	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
470	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
471	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
472	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
473	\begin{proof}
474	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
475	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
476	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
477	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
478	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
479	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
480	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
481	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
482	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
483	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
484	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
485	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
486	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
487	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
488	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
489	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
490	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
491	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
492	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
493	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
494	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
495	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
496	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
497	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
498	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
499	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
500	  exists a unique morphism
501	  \[
502	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
503	  \]
504	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
505	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
506	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
507	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
508	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
509	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
510	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
511	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
512	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
513	  \[
514	    \deg(\varphi)
515	    \;=\;
516	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
517	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
518	  \]
519	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
520	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
521	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
522	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
523	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
524	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
525	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
526	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
527	  and
528	  \[
529	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
530	  \]
531	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
532	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
533	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
534	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
535	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
536	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
537	   C.functionField]} instance). By
538	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
539	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
540	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
541	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
542	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
543	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
544	  closing the headline target
545	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
546	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
547	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
548	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
549	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
550	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
551	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
552	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
553	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
554	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
555	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
556	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
557	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
558	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
559	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
560	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
561	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
562	\end{proof}
563	\begin{remark}
564	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-\(0\) Albanese keystone]
565	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}
566	  Closure of \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} retires the
567	  \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
568	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}
569	  (\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of
570	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr}, body landed iter-166), whose
571	  \emph{first} of two upstream \texttt{sorry} sources is the
572	  \texttt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} target this chapter closes. The
573	  \emph{second} upstream is the helper
574	  \texttt{morphism\_P1\_to\_grpScheme\_const}'s residual sorries, tracked
575	  separately under the genus-\(0\) Albanese arm of \texttt{Jacobian.tex}.
576	\end{remark}
577	\section{Out of scope}
578	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
579	are sequenced elsewhere:
580	\begin{itemize}
581	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) of
582	    genus \(0\) over a \emph{non-closed} field \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point
583	    \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' statement is the \(\bar k \to k\) descent of
584	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}. The descent is hosted in
585	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
586	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} together with Galois descent for
587	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
588	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
589	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
590	    consumed as a black box by
591	    \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
592	    Mathlib (the universal property of \(\mathrm{Proj}\), graded-ring
593	    presentation of \(\mathbb P^n\)) is not re-derived here.
594	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)
595	    is consumed as a black box by \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor};
596	    its proof is a separable sub-lemma threaded inside
597	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} and is
598	    \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4}'s blueprint statement (it lives at
599	    the same logical level as the closely related
600	    \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
601	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
602	    \(\bar k\) with dominant morphisms'' \(\cong\) ``function fields of
603	    transcendence degree~\(1\) over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms''
604	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
605	    \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
606	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean
607	    file threads the spelled-out scheme-theoretic ``finite-degree-one
608	    morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism''
609	    argument (Stacks~\textbf{0AVX}), which is the minimal-Mathlib-dependence
610	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
611	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
612	    has at least two distinct \(\bar k\)-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
613	    cardinality of \(C(\bar k)\)) is consumed implicitly by the present
614	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
615	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter
616	    does not restate it.
617	  \item Cohomology of the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a curve
618	    (Hartshorne~III.5 + IV.1) is fully consumed via \texttt{RR.3}; in
619	    particular the \(H^1\)-vanishing of \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a genus-\(0\)
620	    curve is part of \texttt{RR.3}'s payload feeding
621	    \texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}, and is not
622	    re-derived here.
623	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	% --- iter-187 plan-phase additions: pins for the iter-187 refactor's
102	% Step 3 + Step 4 sub-helpers (carrierPresheaf functor + sheaf property)
103	% which are the direct-construction substrate the definition below
104	% consumes (via the iter-186 plan-phase `analogies/ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
105	% Decision 3 + Decision 4 recipe). The Lean declarations are produced
106	% by the iter-187 refactor-`ocofp-steps3to5` dispatch. The substantive
107	% mathematical content is in the Definition block below; these helper
108	% blocks pin the substrate hooks the prover-side construction uses.
